title: Security review - tier selection, logging/retention, data handling (Arcadia)
preview:
Fireflies.ai - Transcript

Title: Security review - tier selection, logging/retention, data handling (Arcadia)
Date: 2026-03-05
Time: 16:00 - 16:46 UTC
Duration: 46 minutes

Attendees:
- Nikhil Sharma (Redwood Inference, PM Hosted API)
- Rafael Mendes (Redwood Inference, SRE)
- Allison Grant (Redwood Inference, Observability)
- Maya Chen (Arcadia, Security Engineering)
- Rob Keller (Arcadia, Platform Engineering)
- Sanjay Iyer (Arcadia, DPO)
- Elena Petrova (Arcadia, Procurement)

---
Summary (auto-generated):
Arcadia asked whether Redwood’s new batching tier selection (fast/standard/cost-efficient) impacts logging, data retention, or how customer prompts are handled. Redwood confirmed tier selection only changes runtime scheduling/batching behavior and performance telemetry labels; it does not change what is logged, where data is stored, or retention defaults. The teams reviewed Redwood’s logging controls, audit logs, and how tier reason codes show up in metrics without increasing sensitive data exposure.

Topics:
1) Tier selection and data handling
2) Logging/retention defaults and options
3) Audit logs, access controls, and SIEM
4) Telemetry fields (tier label, reason codes) and cardinality controls

Questions:
- Does selecting cost-efficient tier mean more request content is logged?
- Do reason codes include any prompt text or PII?
- Can we disable request payload logging entirely?
- Does streaming change what gets retained?

---
Transcript:

[00:00] Nikhil Sharma: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the security review you requested. Main thing on the agenda is the batching tiers feature and whether it impacts logging, retention, data handling. We’ve got Rafael from SRE and Allison from observability.

[00:16] Maya Chen: Yep, thanks. We’re evaluating your Hosted API right now, and we saw the note about “fast, standard, cost-efficient” tiers. Our concern is basically, does changing a tier change what you collect? Like, do you log more to optimize cost, or does it change retention.

[00:33] Rob Keller: Yeah, and also any new headers or tags you add, if they get shipped to logs, we just want to understand the data footprint.

[00:42] Nikhil Sharma: Totally. Short answer: tier selection only changes runtime behavior—batching, queueing, admission control knobs. It does not change what prompt content is logged, it does not change retention settings, and it does not change where data is processed.

[00:58] Sanjay Iyer: When you say “does not change logging,” can we be precise. Like do you have any tier-specific debug logs or sampling that turns on under cost-efficient.

[01:09] Rafael Mendes: Good question. There are two buckets: (1) request/response payload logging, and (2) operational telemetry like metrics and traces. The tier feature adds labels in operational telemetry—like tier=fast/standard/cost-efficient and a reason code for why a profile was selected. It doesn’t cause us to start capturing payloads if we weren’t already.

[01:31] Maya Chen: So that reason code, does it include any customer data? Like prompt length, or some hash.

[01:38] Allison Grant: It’s not prompt text. The reason code is a fixed enum. Things like “tier_default”, “route_override”, “dedicated_tenant_override”, “model_constraint”, “overload_mode”, that kind of stuff. Prompt length may be used internally for the scheduler decision, but the emitted reason code is not “your prompt was X” or anything like that.

[01:58] Rob Keller: But you said you add labels in metrics—would prompt length be a label.

[02:03] Allison Grant: No. We explicitly avoid high-cardinality labels like prompt length or user ID. Prompt length is bucketed internally as a feature, but the label set is limited. The tier label is one of three values, and reason codes are a small bounded set. We had a canary earlier where cardinality was too high and we fixed it—so it’s constrained.

[02:25] Elena Petrova: Sorry, can I jump in. From procurement side, we just need to document “no change to data retention”. Can you confirm retention is configurable or what the default is.

[02:34] Nikhil Sharma: Yep. For Hosted API, we have standard operational logs and metrics retention. We can share the exact default ranges in the security pack—some are short, like a few days for certain high-volume logs, longer for audit logs. Tier selection doesn’t change those defaults.

[02:52] Sanjay Iyer: Another angle: cost-efficient tier implies more batching. Does that change how long prompts sit in memory, or in buffers, or get written somewhere.

[03:03] Rafael Mendes: It changes time in queue, potentially by milliseconds to tens of milliseconds depending on tier. That’s in-memory queueing in the runtime. It does not write prompts to disk. The same in-memory request object exists regardless; the tier just changes batching windows and admission.

[03:19] Maya Chen: Okay. We also saw “streaming vs non-streaming” affects how it chooses the profile. Does streaming cause you to store partial outputs.

[03:29] Nikhil Sharma: Streaming changes response delivery semantics, but not retention policy. We don’t store partial outputs as a special case due to tier. Any optional debug logging is separate and controlled, not tied to tier.

[03:44] Rob Keller: Let me be blunt. Some vendors do “for optimization we log prompts” unless you opt out. Are you doing any of that.

[03:52] Nikhil Sharma: For Redwood, payload logging is not turned on by default for Hosted in the sense of storing raw prompts for product improvement. We can provide the precise statement from our DPA and security docs. Tier selection doesn’t override any of those controls.

[04:11] Sanjay Iyer: “Not turned on by default” is still a little squishy. What do you log today at request level.

[04:19] Rafael Mendes: At request level, we log metadata needed for operating the service: timestamps, request ID, model, region, status code, latency, token counts, sometimes truncated error messages. We do not need prompt content to run the service. If there is an error, sometimes a small excerpt could appear in an exception trace in some systems—our policy is to prevent that and scrub, but I want to be careful and say we treat prompt/response as customer data and we avoid storing it.

[04:49] Maya Chen: If there is an excerpt in exception traces, that’s exactly the thing. Does tier change error rate or cause more errors that then cause more accidental logging.

[05:00] Rafael Mendes: Tier shouldn’t change correctness. But it can change latency and overload behavior. We’re adding guardrails and reason codes so we can diagnose without extra payload. There’s no plan to increase error logging content based on tier.

[05:17] Allison Grant: And the new reason codes are specifically to avoid people turning on verbose logging. So the idea is: you can see “overload_mode” without capturing request bodies.

[05:28] Rob Keller: Overload mode—what happens there.

[05:31] Rafael Mendes: Under overload, the runtime may apply backpressure, admission control, or load shedding depending on tier. Fast tier tends to protect tail latency with tighter wait budgets, and cost-efficient might allow more queueing or batching. Again, all of that is runtime scheduling. It doesn’t change what data is emitted, other than tags saying it’s in overload mode.

[05:54] Maya Chen: Would those overload tags appear in the same metrics pipeline we receive.

[05:58] Allison Grant: If you’re consuming metrics via our Console, you’d see it. If you’re exporting to your SIEM—typically SIEM is audit logs and security events, not operational metrics. But we can talk about what you ingest.

[06:12] Rob Keller: We’d likely want audit logs and maybe some request logs for billing reconciliation. Not prompt content.

[06:20] Nikhil Sharma: That aligns. Audit logs include events like API key created/rotated, policy changes, role changes, things like that.

[06:31] Sanjay Iyer: Does selecting a tier create an audit event.

[06:36] Allison Grant: Selecting a tier as a configuration change at route/policy level could be an audit event (like “policy updated”). But per-request tier selection—if you do it via header—would not be an audit log entry per request. That would be too noisy and also not a security event.

[06:55] Maya Chen: Wait, you support per-request tier selection.

[06:58] Nikhil Sharma: There are a couple ways, yes. Default is route-level policy. Some customers want request-level overrides. But for your use case, most likely you’d set it at route level.

[07:11] Rob Keller: If it’s request header, does that header get logged.

[07:16] Rafael Mendes: In normal access logs, we don’t log all headers. We log a limited set. We can confirm exactly which ones. Tier might appear as a derived field, not necessarily as raw header.

[07:28] Maya Chen: Derived field meaning “tier=fast” is logged.

[07:31] Allison Grant: In operational telemetry, yes, as a label. In access logs, typically no, unless you enable extended logging. We can align on that.

[07:42] Elena Petrova: For our internal checklist, can you state: “tier selection does not affect logging, retention, or data residency.”

[07:51] Nikhil Sharma: Yes, we can say tier selection does not affect data residency. It’s still processed in the region you call. Tier doesn’t move it.

[08:02] Sanjay Iyer: Data residency aside, what about “training”. Some providers use prompts for training unless opted out.

[08:11] Nikhil Sharma: Redwood does not use customer prompts to train foundation models. We’re an inference platform. For quality improvements we may do aggregated performance analysis, but not training on customer content. That’s in the DPA.

[08:28] Maya Chen: Good. Can we talk about retention controls. If we go Hosted, can we get shorter retention.

[08:35] Rafael Mendes: Potentially, yes. There are constraints because we need some retention for abuse detection and operational debugging, but we offer options particularly for enterprise. Dedicated and Private give you more control.

[08:51] Rob Keller: We’re not doing Private day one. But we might do Dedicated if security wants isolation.

[08:58] Nikhil Sharma: Dedicated gives you isolated GPU pools and more policy control. Tier behavior is the same conceptually, but you can override profiles more explicitly.

[09:10] Maya Chen: Dedicated also means the logs are separate.

[09:13] Rafael Mendes: Operational data plane logs are isolated per tenant in the sense of access controls and separation. Again, we’ll share specifics.

[09:24] Sanjay Iyer: Back to the original question: Does cost-efficient tier increase any sampling. Like do you sample more traces.

[09:33] Allison Grant: No, tracing sampling is controlled by separate knobs. Tier adds a tag on the span if a trace exists. It doesn’t create traces or increase sampling rates.

[09:47] Maya Chen: But you said “why this profile”. That sounds like a more detailed explanation. Where does that live.

[09:55] Allison Grant: In Console, it’s an operator-facing explanation. It’s composed from the reason code and config precedence. Example: “Selected standard (route policy). Override not set. Model constraint: max_batch_tokens limited to X.” It’s still not prompt content.

[10:15] Rob Keller: Model constraint: is that due to quantization.

[10:18] Nikhil Sharma: Sometimes. Some quantized variants have constraints. But again, not customer data.

[10:25] Maya Chen: You mentioned precedence. Can you spell it out.

[10:29] Nikhil Sharma: In general it’s customer override > route policy > tier default, with some safety overrides under overload. If you’re Dedicated, you might have tenant override as top-level.

[10:44] Rob Keller: Overload safety override sounds like it could change behavior without us changing config.

[10:49] Rafael Mendes: Correct, behavior might degrade under overload, but that’s for reliability. It’s more like “we stop accepting requests” or “we apply stricter admission.” It’s not “we log more data.”

[11:05] Maya Chen: We also need to know: do you store prompts at rest anywhere, even briefly.

[11:11] Rafael Mendes: In the course of handling a request, data is in memory. Some systems may use ephemeral disk for swap in the worst case, but our service is configured to avoid that. We can provide a formal statement: we do not persist prompts to disk as part of normal operation.

[11:31] Sanjay Iyer: Okay. And the tier labels, do they end up in billing exports.

[11:37] Nikhil Sharma: They can. The plan is to show cost and latency per tier in Console. That’s for transparency. But it’s still aggregated metrics, not request payload.

[11:50] Rob Keller: We actually like that, to see whether cost-efficient is saving money. But we want to ensure it’s not increasing data exposure.

[12:00] Allison Grant: Understood. Tier is a dimension, like “region” or “model.” We treat it the same way.

[12:08] Maya Chen: Any changes to sub-processors because of this feature.

[12:13] Nikhil Sharma: No. Same infra, same hosting providers. It’s a runtime scheduler feature.

[12:22] Elena Petrova: We’re also doing a compliance mapping. Are you SOC 2.

[12:26] Nikhil Sharma: Yes, SOC 2 Type II. We can share under NDA. And we have ISO alignment work. We’ll send the security pack.

[12:39] Maya Chen: Another nit: sometimes “fast tier” implies “real-time”. If a team flips it, could it bypass DLP rules.

[12:48] Rafael Mendes: No. DLP, WAF, auth checks are independent. Tier is evaluated after auth at policy stage and then used by scheduler. It’s not a bypass.

[13:03] Rob Keller: Okay, I think we’re good on that. Can you show us where in docs it states tiers don’t affect logging.

[13:12] Nikhil Sharma: There will be a public FAQ. For now I’ll email you a written statement and point you to the security documentation. We can also include it in the pilot plan.

[13:26] Maya Chen: We also want to know whether the tier header is considered customer-controlled input and if it shows up in audit.

[13:33] Allison Grant: It’s customer-controlled, yes. It won’t appear in audit logs per request. But if you change a route policy to default to cost-efficient, that config change is auditable.

[13:48] Sanjay Iyer: Good.

[13:50] Rafael Mendes: One nuance: metrics and traces are internal. If you enable export, then yes, the tier label can be exported. But again that’s not sensitive content.

[14:02] Rob Keller: We have a rule about not exporting PII. Tier label is fine.

[14:06] Maya Chen: Can tier reason codes ever include a request ID.

[14:11] Allison Grant: We do have request IDs for debugging, but that’s separate. The reason code is not “request_id=...”. It’s an enum. You may see request ID in access logs if you have them.

[14:25] Maya Chen: Okay.

[14:26] Nikhil Sharma: Maybe helpful if we do a quick recap and then open questions.

[14:31] Nikhil Sharma: Recap: tier selection changes batching / scheduler defaults. It does not change data handling, does not change retention, does not add payload logging, does not change subprocessors. It adds a low-cardinality label and bounded reason codes in operational telemetry.

[14:53] Maya Chen: That addresses the core. But we need a written response. Ideally something we can paste into our risk review.

[15:00] Nikhil Sharma: Yep, I’ll send that after the call.

[15:05] Elena Petrova: Also include the retention defaults and options. Our template asks “how long are logs kept” and “can it be reduced”.

[15:12] Rafael Mendes: We’ll provide. We may need to distinguish audit logs vs service logs vs metrics.

[15:19] Rob Keller: Could you also clarify if the tier is stored in the request record for debugging.

[15:25] Rafael Mendes: It can be present in internal event logs as a field, like “effective_tier=standard”, but again it’s not payload. And it’s governed by the same retention.

[15:38] Maya Chen: That’s fine.

[15:40] Sanjay Iyer: Last one from me. Does cost-efficient tier imply you do longer caching. Like prefix caching might hold data longer.

[15:50] Rafael Mendes: KV cache / prefix cache lives in GPU memory. It’s transient. Tier may make caching more effective because batching patterns change, but it doesn’t change cache TTL policies in a way that persists customer data to disk. It’s memory resident.

[16:08] Sanjay Iyer: Any chance of cross-tenant leakage through cache.

[16:12] Rafael Mendes: No, cache entries are scoped to the runtime instance and request context; for Hosted we isolate at service level; for Dedicated it’s on your isolated pool. We also do not reuse KV cache across tenants in a way that could cause leakage.

[16:30] Maya Chen: Thanks.

[16:32] Rob Keller: This all makes sense. Side note, we’re comparing to Bedrock and Azure OpenAI. They also have performance tiers. Our legal asks whether those tiers change logging. It’s basically the same question.

[16:46] Nikhil Sharma: Yep, common question.

[16:48] Allison Grant: One more nuance: because tier is a tag, dashboards will show it. If you do screenshot sharing internally, that’s fine. Just making sure you know where it appears.

[17:00] Maya Chen: That’s okay.

[17:02] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Next steps: We’ll send you the security pack link, a written statement about tier selection and logging/retention, and Allison can send a short snippet about the reason codes and label set.

[17:16] Elena Petrova: And a follow-up meeting?

[17:18] Nikhil Sharma: If you want, we can do a 30-minute on audit logs and SIEM export. Up to you.

[17:26] Rob Keller: I’d like that.

[17:28] Maya Chen: Same.

[17:30] Nikhil Sharma: Great, we’ll coordinate.

[17:34] (The conversation continues with minor scheduling and email confirmations; no additional technical/security topics were raised.)

[45:58] Nikhil Sharma: Alright, thanks everyone. We’ll follow up by email. Talk soon.

---
Notes:
- Customer’s primary concern was tier selection potentially enabling extra payload logging or changing retention. Redwood clarified tier feature only affects runtime scheduling and emits bounded, non-sensitive telemetry fields (tier + reason codes).
- Requested artifacts: written statement for risk review, retention defaults/options, SOC 2 access instructions, and a follow-up on audit logs/SIEM.

[12:22] Elena Petrova: We’re also doing a compliance mapping. Are you SOC 2.

[12:26] Nikhil Sharma: Yes, SOC 2 Type II. We can share under NDA. And we have ISO alignment work. We’ll send the security pack.

[12:39] Maya Chen: Another nit: sometimes “fast tier” implies “real-time”. If a team flips it, could it bypass DLP rules.

[12:48] Rafael Mendes: No. DLP, WAF, auth checks are independent. Tier is evaluated after auth at policy stage and then used by scheduler. It’s not a bypass.

[13:03] Rob Keller: Okay, I think we’re good on that. Can you show us where in docs it states tiers don’t affect logging.

[13:12] Nikhil Sharma: There will be a public FAQ. For now I’ll email you a written statement and point you to the security documentation. We can also include it in the pilot plan.

[13:26] Maya Chen: We also want to know whether the tier header is considered customer-controlled input and if it shows up in audit.

[13:33] Allison Grant: It’s customer-controlled, yes. It won’t appear in audit logs per request. But if you change a route policy to default to cost-efficient, that config change is auditable.

[13:48] Sanjay Iyer: Good.

[13:50] Rafael Mendes: One nuance: metrics and traces are internal. If you enable export, then yes, the tier label can be exported. But again that’s not sensitive content.

[14:02] Rob Keller: We have a rule about not exporting PII. Tier label is fine.

[14:06] Maya Chen: Can tier reason codes ever include a request ID.

[14:11] Allison Grant: We do have request IDs for debugging, but that’s separate. The reason code is not “request_id=...”. It’s an enum. You may see request ID in access logs if you have them.

[14:25] Maya Chen: Okay.

[14:26] Nikhil Sharma: Maybe helpful if we do a quick recap and then open questions.

[14:31] Nikhil Sharma: Recap: tier selection changes batching / scheduler defaults. It does not change data handling, does not change retention, does not add payload logging, does not change subprocessors. It adds a low-cardinality label and bounded reason codes in operational telemetry.

[14:53] Maya Chen: That addresses the core. But we need a written response. Ideally something we can paste into our risk review.

[15:00] Nikhil Sharma: Yep, I’ll send that after the call.

[15:05] Elena Petrova: Also include the retention defaults and options. Our template asks “how long are logs kept” and “can it be reduced”.

[15:12] Rafael Mendes: We’ll provide. We may need to distinguish audit logs vs service logs vs metrics.

[15:19] Rob Keller: Could you also clarify if the tier is stored in the request record for debugging.

[15:25] Rafael Mendes: It can be present in internal event logs as a field, like “effective_tier=standard”, but again it’s not payload. And it’s governed by the same retention.

[15:38] Maya Chen: That’s fine.

[15:40] Sanjay Iyer: Last one from me. Does cost-efficient tier imply you do longer caching. Like prefix caching might hold data longer.

[15:50] Rafael Mendes: KV cache / prefix cache lives in GPU memory. It’s transient. Tier may make caching more effective because batching patterns change, but it doesn’t change cache TTL policies in a way that persists customer data to disk. It’s memory resident.

[16:08] Sanjay Iyer: Any chance of cross-tenant leakage through cache.

[16:12] Rafael Mendes: No, cache entries are scoped to the runtime instance and request context; for Hosted we isolate at service level; for Dedicated it’s on your isolated pool. We also do not reuse KV cache across tenants in a way that could cause leakage.

[16:30] Maya Chen: Thanks.

[16:32] Rob Keller: This all makes sense. Side note, we’re comparing to Bedrock and Azure OpenAI. They also have performance tiers. Our legal asks whether those tiers change logging. It’s basically the same question.

[16:46] Nikhil Sharma: Yep, common question.

[16:48] Allison Grant: One more nuance: because tier is a tag, dashboards will show it. If you do screenshot sharing internally, that’s fine. Just making sure you know where it appears.

[17:00] Maya Chen: That’s okay.

[17:02] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Next steps: We’ll send you the security pack link, a written statement about tier selection and logging/retention, and Allison can send a short snippet about the reason codes and label set.

[17:16] Elena Petrova: And a follow-up meeting?

[17:18] Nikhil Sharma: If you want, we can do a 30-minute on audit logs and SIEM export. Up to you.

[17:26] Rob Keller: I’d like that.

[17:28] Maya Chen: Same.

[17:30] Nikhil Sharma: Great, we’ll coordinate.

[17:34] (The conversation continues with minor scheduling and email confirmations; no additional technical/security topics were raised.)

[45:58] Nikhil Sharma: Alright, thanks everyone. We’ll follow up by email. Talk soon.

---
Notes:
- Customer’s primary concern was tier selection potentially enabling extra payload logging or changing retention. Redwood clarified tier feature only affects runtime scheduling and emits bounded, non-sensitive telemetry fields (tier + reason codes).
- Requested artifacts: written statement for risk review, retention defaults/options, SOC 2 access instructions, and a follow-up on audit logs/SIEM.

title: POC scoping - FP8/INT8 serving (H100/A100) + rollout plan
preview:
Fireflies.ai - Transcript

Title: POC scoping - FP8/INT8 serving (H100/A100) + rollout plan
Date: 2026-02-25
Time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Avery Johnson (AE), Markus Klein (Solutions Eng), Rafael Mendes (SRE)
Summit AI: Nina Shah (Platform Eng), Ethan Brooks (ML Infra), Luis Martinez (Eng Mgr), Jae Park (Applied ML)

Summary (auto-generated)
- Summit wants a short POC to validate FP8 and INT8 kernels improve end-to-end latency/throughput on their primary chat + summarization traffic without unacceptable quality regressions.
- The group aligned on target models (Llama 3.1 70B class first, one MoE second), target GPU environment (H100 preferred, A100 as secondary), and a benchmark plan that includes both microbench and serving-level workloads with realistic prefill/decode mixes.
- Redwood described their canary rollout approach: feature flags by model/region/cohort, perf-canary thresholds on TTFT/TPOT/tokens-per-sec and error rate, and automatic rollback.

Topics
1) Current stack + what Summit is trying to prove
2) POC success criteria (perf + cost proxy + quality guardrails)
3) Target models + workload definitions (prompt lengths, gen lengths, concurrency)
4) Environment / GPU details (H100 vs A100, clock pinning, driver/CUDA consistency)
5) Benchmark runner + artifact capture
6) Canary / rollout gating and what “safe” means


Transcript

[00:00] Avery Johnson: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for making time. This is the POC scoping for the FP8 and INT8 serving work. Goal today is like, lock success criteria, what models we’re actually testing, what environment, and then how we run it in a way that’s defensible. Markus and Rafael are on from our side.

[00:18] Nina Shah: Yep. Thanks Avery.

[00:20] Markus Klein: Hey all.

[00:22] Rafael Mendes: Hi.

[00:24] Avery Johnson: Quick round, Nina you’re platform, Ethan infra, Luis EM, Jae applied ML, right?

[00:30] Luis Martinez: Yeah, that’s right.

[00:32] Ethan Brooks: Correct.

[00:33] Jae Park: Yep.

[00:35] Avery Johnson: Great. Nina, do you want to restate in your words what you need to see for this to be a “yes”?

[00:41] Nina Shah: Yeah. So, we’re at the point where we can’t really justify moving unless we see a real improvement on H100. We’ve got customers complaining about p95 latency. The like, raw token throughput is one thing, but our pain is mostly time-to-first-token and then kind of tail on decode when concurrency goes up.

[01:03] Nina Shah: We’ve done some vLLM tuning ourselves, and we’re evaluating a couple options. We keep hearing FP8 is basically free wins but then some people say quality gets weird, and INT8 is like, maybe more fragile. So the POC is: prove speedup, and prove we have a safety mechanism.

[01:22] Avery Johnson: Makes sense. Jae, anything to add on “quality gets weird”?

[01:26] Jae Park: Yeah. We had a bad experience with aggressive quant a year ago. Not exactly the same stack, but we saw summarization become kinda… it would miss details. It wasn’t catastrophic but enough that product noticed. So I need a crisp definition of “no regression” or at least bounded regression and how we’ll catch it.

[01:44] Markus Klein: Yep. That’s consistent with what we see too. For us, we treat FP8 and INT8 as separate lanes. FP8 often has very low deltas, but there are model- and context-length-specific gotchas. INT8 can be great on matmuls but you need calibration and guardrails.

[02:04] Avery Johnson: Cool. Let’s talk concrete POC structure. What we typically do is: baseline run in FP16 / BF16 (whatever you consider the current baseline), then run FP8 kernels and INT8 kernels where supported, same workload definitions. Then we also do a quick quality sanity suite.

[02:23] Luis Martinez: Is it like a week, two weeks?

[02:25] Avery Johnson: We can do a one-week POC if environment is ready, but realistically it’s 10 business days if you want stable numbers and a canary-style plan.

[02:36] Ethan Brooks: Environment is always the bottleneck.

[02:38] Avery Johnson: Yeah, exactly.

[02:40] Markus Klein: Maybe we start by locking: models, GPUs, and “what does a pass look like.”

[02:47] Nina Shah: Yep.

[02:49] Markus Klein: On models, in earlier emails you mentioned Llama 70B class and maybe Mixtral?

[02:55] Ethan Brooks: We serve Llama 3.1 70B Instruct mostly. And then we have an MoE route, currently Mixtral 8x7B, but we might move to an 8x22B-ish later. For the POC I’d do Llama 70B first, then one MoE.

[03:14] Markus Klein: Great. So target models: (1) Llama 3.1 70B Instruct, (2) Mixtral 8x7B Instruct. Any third? Like embeddings doesn’t matter here.

[03:24] Nina Shah: Keep it to two. If we can’t prove it on the main one, it’s moot.

[03:29] Avery Johnson: Love it. GPUs: you said H100 is primary. Do you have H100 SXM or PCIe?

[03:35] Ethan Brooks: We have both, but limited. Our main prod is H100 PCIe in us-east-1. We also have some A100 still in eu-west, but that’s not where we want to land.

[03:50] Markus Klein: Okay. For clean benchmarking we really want to avoid shared nodes and avoid MIG if possible.

[03:56] Ethan Brooks: MIG is off for these.

[03:58] Markus Klein: Great.

[04:00] Rafael Mendes: And for the rollout-like plan, the node isolation matters because otherwise tail metrics jump around and you can’t tell if it’s the kernel or noisy neighbors.

[04:10] Nina Shah: Right.

[04:12] Avery Johnson: Success criteria. Let me propose something and we can iterate. For performance, we typically look at:
- p50 and p95 TTFT (time to first token)
- p50 and p95 TPOT (time per output token)
- tokens/sec at a fixed concurrency, plus at your target QPS
- error rate/timeouts

[04:33] Avery Johnson: And a cost proxy like “tokens per GPU-second” just to compare efficiency.

[04:38] Luis Martinez: We do care about cost but yeah, POC is more “can it meet SLO.”

[04:43] Nina Shah: Our current SLO is p95 TTFT under 800ms for chat. We’re not meeting it consistently. For decode, our TPOT target is around 35ms, but it depends on the length.

[04:58] Markus Klein: When you say 35ms, that’s like per token at p95?

[05:02] Nina Shah: Yes, p95-ish.

[05:05] Ethan Brooks: But note, this is at concurrency 8 to 16 per GPU, depending.

[05:11] Markus Klein: Great, that’s important.

[05:13] Avery Johnson: So POC “win” could be: at the same concurrency and same prompt distribution, p95 TTFT improves by, say, 15% or more, and TPOT improves by 10%+ with no increase in error rate. Does that feel too aggressive?

[05:29] Luis Martinez: Not too aggressive. If it’s less than 10% we can’t justify the risk.

[05:34] Nina Shah: Agree.

[05:36] Jae Park: I’d add: quality delta has to be below some threshold.

[05:41] Avery Johnson: Totally. How do we measure quality for this POC? Do you have an eval harness?

[05:47] Jae Park: We have a small golden set. It’s not huge. Like 200 prompts that represent summarization, extraction, and chat. We can run simple scoring: exact match on structured extraction, and for summarization we do like, pairwise preference with a small model judge. It’s not perfect.

[06:09] Markus Klein: That’s plenty for a gating check.

[06:12] Jae Park: I don’t want to bless anything based on vibes.

[06:15] Markus Klein: Yeah. What we typically do is: for FP8, we expect “no statistically meaningful regression” on that set, but we’ll accept tiny noise. For INT8, we sometimes require calibration per model and also we gate by context length.

[06:30] Nina Shah: Context length is a big one. We have long prompts sometimes.

[06:34] Markus Klein: What’s “long” for you? 8k? 32k?

[06:38] Ethan Brooks: Mostly 8k and below, but we have a tail at 16k. Not 32k yet.

[06:45] Markus Klein: Great. We should include some 16k cases in the benchmark suite so we don’t optimize for only short prompts.

[06:52] Avery Johnson: Ok, so success criteria draft:
1) Perf: +15% p95 TTFT improvement and +10% p95 TPOT improvement for Llama 70B at target concurrency, compared to your baseline.
2) Reliability: error rate not worse (timeouts, OOMs).
3) Quality: no meaningful regression on your golden set; if any regression is found, we document it and show gating/fallback.

[07:17] Luis Martinez: Put that in writing and we can sign off.

[07:19] Avery Johnson: Will do.

[07:21] Markus Klein: Workloads. We should define at least two:
- Chat-style: mixed prompt lengths, medium generation lengths.
- Summarization: longer prompts, shorter output.

[07:33] Nina Shah: That matches our traffic.

[07:35] Ethan Brooks: We also have some tool calling. Like JSON schema output.

[07:39] Markus Klein: Ok. That tends to impact decode patterns a little. For POC, we can approximate by forcing structured output / constrained decoding mode if you do that. If not, we can just include prompts that produce JSON.

[07:54] Jae Park: The content matters less than the distribution.

[07:58] Markus Klein: Exactly.

[08:00] Avery Johnson: Environment and how we run this. Our plan is to provide benchmark scripts. We have a matrix runner that can run microbench and end-to-end serving benchmarks and it exports artifacts with metadata (git SHA, CUDA version, driver, clocks).

[08:17] Ethan Brooks: Is this something we run in our VPC? Or you run it on your infra?

[08:21] Avery Johnson: We can do either, but for credibility you may want it in your environment. We can also run in our lab to compare, but final numbers for your sign-off should be in your setup.

[08:33] Nina Shah: We prefer to run it ourselves.

[08:35] Markus Klein: Great. Then we need a pinned container and pinned driver if possible. At minimum, same driver/CUDA across runs.

[08:43] Ethan Brooks: We can pin the container image. Driver is… we can pick a nodegroup with the same AMI.

[08:49] Markus Klein: That works.

[08:51] Rafael Mendes: Also if you can lock GPU clocks during the benchmark window, it reduces variance. Not mandatory but helpful.

[08:59] Ethan Brooks: We can. We’ve done it before. There’s always some ops concerns but for a POC node pool we can do it.

[09:07] Nina Shah: We can reserve a clean pool for a couple days.

[09:10] Avery Johnson: Great. On H100 PCIe, do you have a sense of how many GPUs you can spare? Like 8? 16?

[09:17] Ethan Brooks: Probably 8 GPUs dedicated for two days, maybe three. More is tricky.

[09:23] Markus Klein: That’s fine. The matrix doesn’t need huge parallelism if we prioritize cases.

[09:29] Avery Johnson: Let’s prioritize Llama 70B on H100 first, then if time, A100 for comparison, then Mixtral.

[09:37] Luis Martinez: Agree.

[09:39] Markus Klein: Measurement plan. We do warmups, then fixed duration measurement windows. We measure TTFT and TPOT separately. And we’ll do multiple runs to get variance.

[09:52] Nina Shah: How many runs is “multiple”? We don’t want a science project.

[09:56] Markus Klein: Usually 5 runs per case. If variance is low we can do 3. If variance is high we stop and diagnose, because the numbers won’t be useful.

[10:06] Ethan Brooks: What causes variance typically?

[10:09] Markus Klein: Driver mismatches, clock drift, thermal throttling, shared node contention, debug logging accidentally enabled, stuff like that.

[10:18] Rafael Mendes: Also autoscaling events, or other daemonset spikes. We recommend isolating the pool and turning off disruptive updates during the benchmark.

[10:28] Ethan Brooks: OK.

[10:30] Avery Johnson: Now the canary and rollout approach. Rafael can you summarize how we do this in production?

[10:37] Rafael Mendes: Yeah. So in our runtime, new kernels are enabled behind feature flags. Flags can be set per model, per region, and per cohort. We can route a small percentage of traffic to FP8 or INT8 path and compare against baseline.

[10:53] Rafael Mendes: We use perf canary thresholds. The typical set is: p95 TTFT can’t regress more than X%, p95 TPOT can’t regress more than Y%, tokens/sec can’t drop below some floor, and error rate can’t spike. When a threshold is breached for a sustained window, we auto-rollback the flag.

[11:12] Nina Shah: That’s what we want. Like, we can’t be the team that ships a speed improvement but breaks customers.

[11:18] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. And we also tag metrics by model, GPU, and kernel mode so you can see “oh it’s only the 16k prompt tail” or “only H100 PCIe” etc.

[11:30] Ethan Brooks: Can we replicate that in our environment? We have Prometheus/Grafana.

[11:35] Rafael Mendes: We can provide a template. For the POC, simplest is: run controlled load tests and produce a report, but we can also hook up the same metric dimensions. Markus will share what we export.

[11:49] Markus Klein: The benchmark runner exports JSON artifacts and CSV plus optional nsys traces for a few cases.

[11:56] Jae Park: nsys as in Nsight Systems?

[11:59] Markus Klein: Yeah. Optional. Usually only for top anomalies.

[12:04] Avery Johnson: On FP8 vs INT8: for POC, do you want both or do you want FP8 first?

[12:10] Nina Shah: I want both, but if INT8 takes a bunch of calibration and is risky, do FP8 first.

[12:17] Markus Klein: That’s the right order. FP8 attention and some matmul paths can be enabled with shape guardrails. INT8 generally needs calibration and we need to confirm shape support for your exact model variant.

[12:30] Ethan Brooks: Our baseline is BF16 currently.

[12:33] Markus Klein: Perfect. So baseline BF16, compare to FP8, then INT8 where eligible.

[12:40] Jae Park: What’s your fallback behavior when INT8 fails? Like correctness.

[12:44] Markus Klein: We have hard guardrails. If shape constraints aren’t met or alignment is off, we fall back to baseline kernel path automatically. Same if the kernel is disabled for a known bad regime.

[12:58] Rafael Mendes: And operationally, if perf canary sees a regression, we flip the flag off and you’re back to baseline. No redeploy required.

[13:07] Nina Shah: That’s good.

[13:09] Avery Johnson: Timeline. If we can get environment access and a reserved pool by early next week, we can run baseline and FP8 by midweek and have results by end of week. INT8 might extend into the following week if calibration is needed.

[13:26] Luis Martinez: That’s acceptable.

[13:28] Ethan Brooks: What do you need from us to start?

[13:31] Markus Klein: Four things:
1) Confirm GPU details: H100 PCIe in us-east-1, driver version, CUDA container constraints.
2) Your workload distribution: prompt length buckets, output length, concurrency targets.
3) The model artifacts: exact model version and tokenizer settings, plus any custom prompts/system prompts.
4) A place to put artifacts (S3 bucket) or we can give you an uploader.

[14:02] Ethan Brooks: We can do S3.

[14:04] Nina Shah: We can share prompt stats, not the content. We can give length histograms.

[14:09] Markus Klein: That’s enough.

[14:12] Jae Park: For eval, we can run the golden set locally. I don’t want to ship our prompts outside.

[14:19] Avery Johnson: Totally fine. We don’t need the content. Just the scoring results.

[14:25] Luis Martinez: Are you going to ask for a dedicated cluster or can you do hosted?

[14:29] Avery Johnson: For a POC like this, we typically do dedicated capacity so we can control GPU type and avoid noisy neighbors. But since you want to run in your environment, that’s fine too. Long term, if you buy Dedicated from us, you’ll get similar isolation plus our operational guardrails.

[14:48] Nina Shah: For now we just want proof.

[14:51] Avery Johnson: Yep.

[14:53] Ethan Brooks: One question: how do we ensure apples-to-apples between baseline and FP8? Like, scheduler behavior can change.

[15:01] Markus Klein: Great question. We keep scheduler settings fixed for the comparison, then we can do a second phase where we tune scheduler knobs to realize the kernel wins. But for the POC result, we should show both: “raw kernel effect” and “end-to-end tuned.”

[15:18] Nina Shah: But you’ll call out what changed.

[15:20] Markus Klein: Exactly. We record the config. Batching windows, prefill/decode interleaving settings, backpressure thresholds.

[15:29] Rafael Mendes: And for safety, we’d only roll one change at a time into canary. Kernel mode first, then scheduler tuning. Separate flags.

[15:37] Luis Martinez: That’s what I want to hear.

[15:40] Avery Johnson: Any objections to a two-phase POC? Phase A: baseline vs FP8 with fixed scheduler. Phase B: FP8 plus scheduler tuning if needed.

[15:51] Nina Shah: That’s fine as long as Phase A already shows improvement. If Phase A is flat, we probably stop.

[15:57] Avery Johnson: Fair.

[16:00] Jae Park: For quality gating, can we agree now what constitutes “regression”? Like, for extraction, exact match; for summarization, preference scores.

[16:09] Markus Klein: We can put in writing: no more than, say, 1% drop on structured extraction exact-match on your golden set. For summarization preference, we can accept within noise bands. You tell us what noise looks like in your harness.

[16:25] Jae Park: We usually treat a 2-3 point drop as meaningful. But we can calibrate.

[16:31] Avery Johnson: Let’s define it as: “no statistically significant drop” and also “no single category drops more than X.” We’ll let you set X.

[16:41] Luis Martinez: Make it simple. We can’t debate stats for a week.

[16:45] Avery Johnson: Okay. Jae, you pick thresholds, we’ll align.

[16:50] Jae Park: Deal.

[16:52] Ethan Brooks: Back to environment. Are you requiring a specific CUDA? We’re on 12.2 right now.

[16:57] Markus Klein: 12.2 is fine. We’ll send the pinned tags. The key is consistency across runs.

[17:05] Ethan Brooks: And driver?

[17:07] Markus Klein: As long as it’s compatible with CUDA 12.2 and stable. If you have multiple clusters with different drivers, don’t mix them in the same matrix.

[17:18] Ethan Brooks: Got it.

[17:20] Avery Johnson: Let’s talk about outputs. At the end of the POC, we want a short readout: table of p50/p95 TTFT, p50/p95 TPOT, throughput at concurrency targets, plus variance, plus a quality note.

[17:35] Nina Shah: And cost proxy.

[17:37] Avery Johnson: Yep. Tokens per GPU second or similar.

[17:41] Luis Martinez: And a rollout plan: how we would enable it safely.

[17:45] Rafael Mendes: We can provide that. Usually: start with 1 region, 1 model, 1% traffic, monitor 2 hours, then 5%, then 25%, then 50. With hard rollback triggers.

[17:57] Nina Shah: We’d probably do even slower.

[17:59] Rafael Mendes: Totally fine.

[18:02] Markus Klein: For benchmark cases, I’d propose:
- Chat: prompt length buckets 128, 512, 2k, 8k (and some 16k), output length 128-512 tokens.
- Summarization: prompts 4k-16k, output 128-256.
- Concurrency: 1, 4, 8, 16 per GPU (or whatever maps to your routing).

[18:27] Ethan Brooks: That matches.

[18:29] Nina Shah: Our typical output length is maybe 200 tokens. But we do have long chat sometimes.

[18:35] Markus Klein: We can include a 1k decode case for tail.

[18:39] Jae Park: That’s also where quality can drift.

[18:42] Markus Klein: Yep.

[18:44] Avery Johnson: Any security blockers? Like, do we need to sign anything for running scripts?

[18:49] Nina Shah: Not for scripts. But we can’t share prompt data. And we’d prefer minimal outbound network.

[18:56] Markus Klein: Understood. Runner can work without sending data out. Artifact upload to your S3 only.

[19:05] Ethan Brooks: Great.

[19:07] Avery Johnson: Cool. I think we’re aligned. Let’s do a quick recap and confirm owners.

[19:12] Avery Johnson: From Redwood: I’ll send a POC outline email with success criteria draft and timeline. Markus sends the runner and config. Rafael sends canary threshold template.

[19:24] Avery Johnson: From Summit: Nina confirms the reserved H100 pool details and whether you can lock clocks. Ethan shares baseline metrics and driver/CUDA info. Jae defines quality gates on the golden set.

[19:38] Luis Martinez: And schedule the technical kickoff.

[19:41] Avery Johnson: Yep. We’ll send times.

[19:45] Nina Shah: Sounds good.

[19:47] Ethan Brooks: Works.

[19:49] Jae Park: Fine.

[19:51] Avery Johnson: Great. Anything else before we drop?

[19:55] Nina Shah: Just one more. If we see a regression, like TTFT gets worse under FP8 on some prompts, what’s the typical response?

[20:03] Markus Klein: First we confirm it’s not measurement noise. Then we check shape regime. Often it’s a specific prompt length or batch regime where launch overhead dominates. In that case we can disable FP8 for that regime and fall back to baseline. That’s usually a one-line heuristic in the dispatch.

[20:20] Rafael Mendes: And if it happens in canary, rollback is automatic. We treat it as a “no customer impact” event.

[20:27] Nina Shah: Perfect.

[20:29] Avery Johnson: Alright. Thanks all.

[20:32] Call ended (recording stopped)

Notes
- Some crosstalk and a few sections were lightly truncated by the recorder; main decisions and next steps captured above.
- Summit emphasized: p95 tail and safe rollback are as important as average throughput wins.

Goal today is like, lock success criteria, what models we’re actually testing, what environment, and then how we run it in a way that’s defensible. Markus and Rafael are on from our side.

[00:18] Nina Shah: Yep. Thanks Avery.

[00:20] Markus Klein: Hey all.

[00:22] Rafael Mendes: Hi.

[00:24] Avery Johnson: Quick round, Nina you’re platform, Ethan infra, Luis EM, Jae applied ML, right?

[00:30] Luis Martinez: Yeah, that’s right.

[00:32] Ethan Brooks: Correct.

[00:33] Jae Park: Yep.

[00:35] Avery Johnson: Great. Nina, do you want to restate in your words what you need to see for this to be a “yes”?

[00:41] Nina Shah: Yeah. So, we’re at the point where we can’t really justify moving unless we see a real improvement on H100. We’ve got customers complaining about p95 latency. The like, raw token throughput is one thing, but our pain is mostly time-to-first-token and then kind of tail on decode when concurrency goes up.

[01:03] Nina Shah: We’ve done some vLLM tuning ourselves, and we’re evaluating a couple options. We keep hearing FP8 is basically free wins but then some people say quality gets weird, and INT8 is like, maybe more fragile. So the POC is: prove speedup, and prove we have a safety mechanism.

[01:22] Avery Johnson: Makes sense. Jae, anything to add on “quality gets weird”?

[01:26] Jae Park: Yeah. We had a bad experience with aggressive quant a year ago. Not exactly the same stack, but we saw summarization become kinda… it would miss details. It wasn’t catastrophic but enough that product noticed. So I need a crisp definition of “no regression” or at least bounded regression and how we’ll catch it.

[01:44] Markus Klein: Yep. That’s consistent with what we see too. For us, we treat FP8 and INT8 as separate lanes. FP8 often has very low deltas, but there are model- and context-length-specific gotchas. INT8 can be great on matmuls but you need calibration and guardrails.

[02:04] Avery Johnson: Cool. Let’s talk concrete POC structure. What we typically do is: baseline run in FP16 / BF16 (whatever you consider the current baseline), then run FP8 kernels and INT8 kernels where supported, same workload definitions. Then we also do a quick quality sanity suite.

[02:23] Luis Martinez: Is it like a week, two weeks?

[02:25] Avery Johnson: We can do a one-week POC if environment is ready, but realistically it’s 10 business days if you want stable numbers and a canary-style plan.

[02:36] Ethan Brooks: Environment is always the bottleneck.

[02:38] Avery Johnson: Yeah, exactly.

[02:40] Markus Klein: Maybe we start by locking: models, GPUs, and “what does a pass look like.”

[02:47] Nina Shah: Yep.

[02:49] Markus Klein: On models, in earlier emails you mentioned Llama 70B class and maybe Mixtral?

[02:55] Ethan Brooks: We serve Llama 3.1 70B Instruct mostly. And then we have an MoE route, currently Mixtral 8x7B, but we might move to an 8x22B-ish later. For the POC I’d do Llama 70B first, then one MoE.

[03:14] Markus Klein: Great. So target models: (1) Llama 3.1 70B Instruct, (2) Mixtral 8x7B Instruct. Any third? Like embeddings doesn’t matter here.

[03:24] Nina Shah: Keep it to two. If we can’t prove it on the main one, it’s moot.

[03:29] Avery Johnson: Love it. GPUs: you said H100 is primary. Do you have H100 SXM or PCIe?

[03:35] Ethan Brooks: We have both, but limited. Our main prod is H100 PCIe in us-east-1. We also have some A100 still in eu-west, but that’s not where we want to land.

[03:50] Markus Klein: Okay. For clean benchmarking we really want to avoid shared nodes and avoid MIG if possible.

[03:56] Ethan Brooks: MIG is off for these.

[03:58] Markus Klein: Great.

[04:00] Rafael Mendes: And for the rollout-like plan, the node isolation matters because otherwise tail metrics jump around and you can’t tell if it’s the kernel or noisy neighbors.

[04:10] Nina Shah: Right.

[04:12] Avery Johnson: Success criteria. Let me propose something and we can iterate. For performance, we typically look at:
- p50 and p95 TTFT (time to first token)
- p50 and p95 TPOT (time per output token)
- tokens/sec at a fixed concurrency, plus at your target QPS
- error rate/timeouts

[04:33] Avery Johnson: And a cost proxy like “tokens per GPU-second” just to compare efficiency.

[04:38] Luis Martinez: We do care about cost but yeah, POC is more “can it meet SLO.”

[04:43] Nina Shah: Our current SLO is p95 TTFT under 800ms for chat. We’re not meeting it consistently. For decode, our TPOT target is around 35ms, but it depends on the length.

[04:58] Markus Klein: When you say 35ms, that’s like per token at p95?

[05:02] Nina Shah: Yes, p95-ish.

[05:05] Ethan Brooks: But note, this is at concurrency 8 to 16 per GPU, depending.

[05:11] Markus Klein: Great, that’s important.

[05:13] Avery Johnson: So POC “win” could be: at the same concurrency and same prompt distribution, p95 TTFT improves by, say, 15% or more, and TPOT improves by 10%+ with no increase in error rate. Does that feel too aggressive?

[05:29] Luis Martinez: Not too aggressive. If it’s less than 10% we can’t justify the risk.

[05:34] Nina Shah: Agree.

[05:36] Jae Park: I’d add: quality delta has to be below some threshold.

[05:41] Avery Johnson: Totally. How do we measure quality for this POC? Do you have an eval harness?

[05:47] Jae Park: We have a small golden set. It’s not huge. Like 200 prompts that represent summarization, extraction, and chat. We can run simple scoring: exact match on structured extraction, and for summarization we do like, pairwise preference with a small model judge. It’s not perfect.

[06:09] Markus Klein: That’s plenty for a gating check.

[06:12] Jae Park: I don’t want to bless anything based on vibes.

[06:15] Markus Klein: Yeah. What we typically do is: for FP8, we expect “no statistically meaningful regression” on that set, but we’ll accept tiny noise. For INT8, we sometimes require calibration per model and also we gate by context length.

[06:30] Nina Shah: Context length is a big one. We have long prompts sometimes.

[06:34] Markus Klein: What’s “long” for you? 8k? 32k?

[06:38] Ethan Brooks: Mostly 8k and below, but we have a tail at 16k. Not 32k yet.

[06:45] Markus Klein: Great. We should include some 16k cases in the benchmark suite so we don’t optimize for only short prompts.

[06:52] Avery Johnson: Ok, so success criteria draft:
1) Perf: +15% p95 TTFT improvement and +10% p95 TPOT improvement for Llama 70B at target concurrency, compared to your baseline.
2) Reliability: error rate not worse (timeouts, OOMs).
3) Quality: no meaningful regression on your golden set; if any regression is found, we document it and show gating/fallback.

[07:17] Luis Martinez: Put that in writing and we can sign off.

[07:19] Avery Johnson: Will do.

[07:21] Markus Klein: Workloads. We should define at least two:
- Chat-style: mixed prompt lengths, medium generation lengths.
- Summarization: longer prompts, shorter output.

[07:33] Nina Shah: That matches our traffic.

[07:35] Ethan Brooks: We also have some tool calling. Like JSON schema output.

[07:39] Markus Klein: Ok. That tends to impact decode patterns a little. For POC, we can approximate by forcing structured output / constrained decoding mode if you do that. If not, we can just include prompts that produce JSON.

[07:54] Jae Park: The content matters less than the distribution.

[07:58] Markus Klein: Exactly.

[08:00] Avery Johnson: Environment and how we run this. Our plan is to provide benchmark scripts. We have a matrix runner that can run microbench and end-to-end serving benchmarks and it exports artifacts with metadata (git SHA, CUDA version, driver, clocks).

[08:17] Ethan Brooks: Is this something we run in our VPC? Or you run it on your infra?

[08:21] Avery Johnson: We can do either, but for credibility you may want it in your environment. We can also run in our lab to compare, but final numbers for your sign-off should be in your setup.

[08:33] Nina Shah: We prefer to run it ourselves.

[08:35] Markus Klein: Great. Then we need a pinned container and pinned driver if possible. At minimum, same driver/CUDA across runs.

[08:43] Ethan Brooks: We can pin the container image. Driver is… we can pick a nodegroup with the same AMI.

[08:49] Markus Klein: That works.

[08:51] Rafael Mendes: Also if you can lock GPU clocks during the benchmark window, it reduces variance. Not mandatory but helpful.

[08:59] Ethan Brooks: We can. We’ve done it before. There’s always some ops concerns but for a POC node pool we can do it.

[09:07] Nina Shah: We can reserve a clean pool for a couple days.

[09:10] Avery Johnson: Great. On H100 PCIe, do you have a sense of how many GPUs you can spare? Like 8? 16?

[09:17] Ethan Brooks: Probably 8 GPUs dedicated for two days, maybe three. More is tricky.

[09:23] Markus Klein: That’s fine. The matrix doesn’t need huge parallelism if we prioritize cases.

[09:29] Avery Johnson: Let’s prioritize Llama 70B on H100 first, then if time, A100 for comparison, then Mixtral.

[09:37] Luis Martinez: Agree.

[09:39] Markus Klein: Measurement plan. We do warmups, then fixed duration measurement windows. We measure TTFT and TPOT separately. And we’ll do multiple runs to get variance.

[09:52] Nina Shah: How many runs is “multiple”? We don’t want a science project.

[09:56] Markus Klein: Usually 5 runs per case. If variance is low we can do 3. If variance is high we stop and diagnose, because the numbers won’t be useful.

[10:06] Ethan Brooks: What causes variance typically?

[10:09] Markus Klein: Driver mismatches, clock drift, thermal throttling, shared node contention, debug logging accidentally enabled, stuff like that.

[10:18] Rafael Mendes: Also autoscaling events, or other daemonset spikes. We recommend isolating the pool and turning off disruptive updates during the benchmark.

[10:28] Ethan Brooks: OK.

[10:30] Avery Johnson: Now the canary and rollout approach. Rafael can you summarize how we do this in production?

[10:37] Rafael Mendes: Yeah. So in our runtime, new kernels are enabled behind feature flags. Flags can be set per model, per region, and per cohort. We can route a small percentage of traffic to FP8 or INT8 path and compare against baseline.

[10:53] Rafael Mendes: We use perf canary thresholds. The typical set is: p95 TTFT can’t regress more than X%, p95 TPOT can’t regress more than Y%, tokens/sec can’t drop below some floor, and error rate can’t spike. When a threshold is breached for a sustained window, we auto-rollback the flag.

[11:12] Nina Shah: That’s what we want. Like, we can’t be the team that ships a speed improvement but breaks customers.

[11:18] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. And we also tag metrics by model, GPU, and kernel mode so you can see “oh it’s only the 16k prompt tail” or “only H100 PCIe” etc.

[11:30] Ethan Brooks: Can we replicate that in our environment? We have Prometheus/Grafana.

[11:35] Rafael Mendes: We can provide a template. For the POC, simplest is: run controlled load tests and produce a report, but we can also hook up the same metric dimensions. Markus will share what we export.

[11:49] Markus Klein: The benchmark runner exports JSON artifacts and CSV plus optional nsys traces for a few cases.

[11:56] Jae Park: nsys as in Nsight Systems?

[11:59] Markus Klein: Yeah. Optional. Usually only for top anomalies.

[12:04] Avery Johnson: On FP8 vs INT8: for POC, do you want both or do you want FP8 first?

[12:10] Nina Shah: I want both, but if INT8 takes a bunch of calibration and is risky, do FP8 first.

[12:17] Markus Klein: That’s the right order. FP8 attention and some matmul paths can be enabled with shape guardrails. INT8 generally needs calibration and we need to confirm shape support for your exact model variant.

[12:30] Ethan Brooks: Our baseline is BF16 currently.

[12:33] Markus Klein: Perfect. So baseline BF16, compare to FP8, then INT8 where eligible.

[12:40] Jae Park: What’s your fallback behavior when INT8 fails? Like correctness.

[12:44] Markus Klein: We have hard guardrails. If shape constraints aren’t met or alignment is off, we fall back to baseline kernel path automatically. Same if the kernel is disabled for a known bad regime.

[12:58] Rafael Mendes: And operationally, if perf canary sees a regression, we flip the flag off and you’re back to baseline. No redeploy required.

[13:07] Nina Shah: That’s good.

[13:09] Avery Johnson: Timeline. If we can get environment access and a reserved pool by early next week, we can run baseline and FP8 by midweek and have results by end of week. INT8 might extend into the following week if calibration is needed.

[13:26] Luis Martinez: That’s acceptable.

[13:28] Ethan Brooks: What do you need from us to start?

[13:31] Markus Klein: Four things:
1) Confirm GPU details: H100 PCIe in us-east-1, driver version, CUDA container constraints.
2) Your workload distribution: prompt length buckets, output length, concurrency targets.
3) The model artifacts: exact model version and tokenizer settings, plus any custom prompts/system prompts.
4) A place to put artifacts (S3 bucket) or we can give you an uploader.

[14:02] Ethan Brooks: We can do S3.

[14:04] Nina Shah: We can share prompt stats, not the content. We can give length histograms.

[14:09] Markus Klein: That’s enough.

[14:12] Jae Park: For eval, we can run the golden set locally. I don’t want to ship our prompts outside.

[14:19] Avery Johnson: Totally fine. We don’t need the content. Just the scoring results.

[14:25] Luis Martinez: Are you going to ask for a dedicated cluster or can you do hosted?

[14:29] Avery Johnson: For a POC like this, we typically do dedicated capacity so we can control GPU type and avoid noisy neighbors. But since you want to run in your environment, that’s fine too. Long term, if you buy Dedicated from us, you’ll get similar isolation plus our operational guardrails.

[14:48] Nina Shah: For now we just want proof.

[14:51] Avery Johnson: Yep.

[14:53] Ethan Brooks: One question: how do we ensure apples-to-apples between baseline and FP8? Like, scheduler behavior can change.

[15:01] Markus Klein: Great question. We keep scheduler settings fixed for the comparison, then we can do a second phase where we tune scheduler knobs to realize the kernel wins. But for the POC result, we should show both: “raw kernel effect” and “end-to-end tuned.”

[15:18] Nina Shah: But you’ll call out what changed.

[15:20] Markus Klein: Exactly. We record the config. Batching windows, prefill/decode interleaving settings, backpressure thresholds.

[15:29] Rafael Mendes: And for safety, we’d only roll one change at a time into canary. Kernel mode first, then scheduler tuning. Separate flags.

[15:37] Luis Martinez: That’s what I want to hear.

[15:40] Avery Johnson: Any objections to a two-phase POC? Phase A: baseline vs FP8 with fixed scheduler. Phase B: FP8 plus scheduler tuning if needed.

[15:51] Nina Shah: That’s fine as long as Phase A already shows improvement. If Phase A is flat, we probably stop.

[15:57] Avery Johnson: Fair.

[16:00] Jae Park: For quality gating, can we agree now what constitutes “regression”? Like, for extraction, exact match; for summarization, preference scores.

[16:09] Markus Klein: We can put in writing: no more than, say, 1% drop on structured extraction exact-match on your golden set. For summarization preference, we can accept within noise bands. You tell us what noise looks like in your harness.

[16:25] Jae Park: We usually treat a 2-3 point drop as meaningful. But we can calibrate.

[16:31] Avery Johnson: Let’s define it as: “no statistically significant drop” and also “no single category drops more than X.” We’ll let you set X.

[16:41] Luis Martinez: Make it simple. We can’t debate stats for a week.

[16:45] Avery Johnson: Okay. Jae, you pick thresholds, we’ll align.

[16:50] Jae Park: Deal.

[16:52] Ethan Brooks: Back to environment. Are you requiring a specific CUDA? We’re on 12.2 right now.

[16:57] Markus Klein: 12.2 is fine. We’ll send the pinned tags. The key is consistency across runs.

[17:05] Ethan Brooks: And driver?

[17:07] Markus Klein: As long as it’s compatible with CUDA 12.2 and stable. If you have multiple clusters with different drivers, don’t mix them in the same matrix.

[17:18] Ethan Brooks: Got it.

[17:20] Avery Johnson: Let’s talk about outputs. At the end of the POC, we want a short readout: table of p50/p95 TTFT, p50/p95 TPOT, throughput at concurrency targets, plus variance, plus a quality note.

[17:35] Nina Shah: And cost proxy.

[17:37] Avery Johnson: Yep. Tokens per GPU second or similar.

[17:41] Luis Martinez: And a rollout plan: how we would enable it safely.

[17:45] Rafael Mendes: We can provide that. Usually: start with 1 region, 1 model, 1% traffic, monitor 2 hours, then 5%, then 25%, then 50. With hard rollback triggers.

[17:57] Nina Shah: We’d probably do even slower.

[17:59] Rafael Mendes: Totally fine.

[18:02] Markus Klein: For benchmark cases, I’d propose:
- Chat: prompt length buckets 128, 512, 2k, 8k (and some 16k), output length 128-512 tokens.
- Summarization: prompts 4k-16k, output 128-256.
- Concurrency: 1, 4, 8, 16 per GPU (or whatever maps to your routing).

[18:27] Ethan Brooks: That matches.

[18:29] Nina Shah: Our typical output length is maybe 200 tokens. But we do have long chat sometimes.

[18:35] Markus Klein: We can include a 1k decode case for tail.

[18:39] Jae Park: That’s also where quality can drift.

[18:42] Markus Klein: Yep.

[18:44] Avery Johnson: Any security blockers? Like, do we need to sign anything for running scripts?

[18:49] Nina Shah: Not for scripts. But we can’t share prompt data. And we’d prefer minimal outbound network.

[18:56] Markus Klein: Understood. Runner can work without sending data out. Artifact upload to your S3 only.

[19:05] Ethan Brooks: Great.

[19:07] Avery Johnson: Cool. I think we’re aligned. Let’s do a quick recap and confirm owners.

[19:12] Avery Johnson: From Redwood: I’ll send a POC outline email with success criteria draft and timeline. Markus sends the runner and config. Rafael sends canary threshold template.

[19:24] Avery Johnson: From Summit: Nina confirms the reserved H100 pool details and whether you can lock clocks. Ethan shares baseline metrics and driver/CUDA info. Jae defines quality gates on the golden set.

[19:38] Luis Martinez: And schedule the technical kickoff.

[19:41] Avery Johnson: Yep. We’ll send times.

[19:45] Nina Shah: Sounds good.

[19:47] Ethan Brooks: Works.

[19:49] Jae Park: Fine.

[19:51] Avery Johnson: Great. Anything else before we drop?

[19:55] Nina Shah: Just one more. If we see a regression, like TTFT gets worse under FP8 on some prompts, what’s the typical response?

[20:03] Markus Klein: First we confirm it’s not measurement noise. Then we check shape regime. Often it’s a specific prompt length or batch regime where launch overhead dominates. In that case we can disable FP8 for that regime and fall back to baseline. That’s usually a one-line heuristic in the dispatch.

[20:20] Rafael Mendes: And if it happens in canary, rollback is automatic. We treat it as a “no customer impact” event.

[20:27] Nina Shah: Perfect.

[20:29] Avery Johnson: Alright. Thanks all.

[20:32] Call ended (recording stopped)

Notes
- Some crosstalk and a few sections were lightly truncated by the recorder; main decisions and next steps captured above.
- Summit emphasized: p95 tail and safe rollback are as important as average throughput wins.

title: Acme AI x Redwood — Post-Incident Review (Dedicated) — Jan 10 overload/5xx
preview:
Meeting: Acme AI x Redwood — Post-Incident Review (Dedicated) — Jan 10 overload/5xx
Recorded by Fireflies
Date: 2026-01-14
Time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Rafael Mendes, Lauren Bishop, Dev Patel
Acme AI: Jordan Lee, Priya Nair, Miguel Santos, Hannah Wu

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed the Jan 10 Dedicated incident impacting Acme AI (elevated 5xx, latency, and intermittent 429s during a burst window).
- Redwood walked through timeline, mitigations (admission control changes, autoscale ceiling increase, capacity rebalance), and likely root cause (insufficient headroom + routing behavior under saturation).
- Customer feedback focused on earlier warning, clearer status updates, and what remediation SLAs mean for Dedicated.
- Agreed on follow-ups: share PIR, confirm classification, align on comms templates and headroom planning.

Topics (auto-generated)
- Incident timeline and impact
- Dedicated capacity saturation signals
- Router fallback / spillover behavior
- Client retry/backoff and streaming behavior
- Remediation SLAs and customer communications
- Preventative actions and monitoring

Questions (auto-generated)
- When does the mitigation clock start for Dedicated, and what counts as “mitigated”?
- Will Redwood page Acme on-call automatically for future Sev1/Sev0 incidents?
- Can we have a consistent comms template that doesn’t over-promise root cause?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the post-incident review for the January 10th event on your Dedicated cluster. Goal is to walk the timeline, what we did to mitigate, what we think the root cause was, and then spend time on your concerns around comms and SLAs. And we’ll leave with concrete next steps.

[02:52] Rachel Kim: Yep, understood.

[04:28] Rachel Kim: So total customer-visible impact window—about 29 minutes for elevated errors/latency, with the sharpest part in the first ~15.

[04:37] Hannah Wu: That matches our notes.

[05:49] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[07:10] Rachel Kim: And we’ll put that explicitly in the write-up so it’s not ambiguous.

[07:14] Miguel Santos: Okay.

Three is: do we get credits, and how do the SLAs apply to Dedicated.

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yep, all fair.

[08:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly.

[08:46] Miguel Santos: Could you page us directly? We have PagerDuty.

[08:49] Rachel Kim: We can talk about options. Some customers prefer Slack + email. Direct PD paging is possible but we need to coordinate severity thresholds so we don’t page you for every blip.

[09:03] Hannah Wu: We’d want it only for Sev1 and Sev0, I think.

[09:06] Lauren Bishop: That aligns with our thinking.

[10:15] Priya Nair: Good.

[11:57] Rafael Mendes: Noted.

[12:24] Rafael Mendes: Exactly.

[13:29] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[15:10] Priya Nair: We did roll a new version, yes.

[15:33] Rachel Kim: Great.

[16:29] Rafael Mendes: Correct.

[17:18] Rachel Kim: That’s part of the templates.

[17:36] Jordan Lee: Is active-active possible?

[17:38] Lauren Bishop: It’s possible as an add-on architecture, but it’s more cost. Let’s not derail today, but we can do a separate design session.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[17:50] Rachel Kim: I’m looking at time—we have ~10 minutes. I want to make sure we captured your top asks: earlier warning, consistent comms cadence with safer language, clarity on mitigation vs fix, and confidence there was no model fallback. Anything else?

[18:06] Priya Nair: That’s it for me.

[18:07] Miguel Santos: Just make sure the “profile drift” thing gets fixed. That’s important.

[18:12] Jordan Lee: And headroom planning.

[18:14] Rachel Kim: Great.

[18:16] Rafael Mendes: For the record, our internal follow-up will include adding better labels for incident type so we don’t end up with generic “overload” every time. This one should be “Dedicated capacity saturation.”

[18:29] Priya Nair: Yep.

[18:31] Rachel Kim: Cool. Next steps recap: Rafael will send the PIR write-up with timeline/metrics and final classification by Friday. Lauren sends the Dedicated remediation SLA one-pager and how it ties to credits evaluation. Dev sends comms template snippet and cadence expectations. And Acme will send workload forecast and retry/backoff info.

[18:55] Hannah Wu: And a follow-up meeting for the burst window config?

[18:58] Rachel Kim: Yes, we’ll schedule a 30-minute follow-up in two weeks. We’ll include Fleet as needed.

[19:05] Jordan Lee: Works.

[19:06] Priya Nair: Thanks.

[19:07] Miguel Santos: Thank you.

[19:09] Rachel Kim: Thanks everyone. We’ll get those notes out.

(Transcript ends — remainder of recording contained small talk / scheduling that was not fully transcribed.)

[09:41] Miguel Santos: That’s a big deal for us. We A/B on our side and any drift causes false positives.

[09:47] Rafael Mendes: Understood. We have an internal control plane setting to prevent profile variance across the pool; we’re going to enforce that for your cluster.

[09:57] Jordan Lee: So we can say there was no model change, but there may have been a different “profile”?

[10:02] Rafael Mendes: Correct. Same model version. Different execution profile. We’ll write it that way.

[10:10] Rachel Kim: And in customer comms we’ll avoid saying “fallback model” unless it truly was.

[10:15] Priya Nair: Good.

[10:16] Rachel Kim: Lauren, can you cover credits / SLA?

[10:20] Lauren Bishop: Yep. For Dedicated, your contract has an uptime commitment and a support response commitment. The remediation SLAs we’re rolling out are operational targets—time to mitigate/fix—used to drive internal behavior and reporting. They’re not automatically the same as contractual credits, but they should map sensibly.

[10:41] Jordan Lee: So are we eligible for credits from this event?

[10:44] Lauren Bishop: We’ll evaluate it against the SLA definition in your order form: downtime minutes and measurement method. This incident looks like partial unavailability rather than full outage; we need to quantify. Rachel and Dev will follow up with the calculation approach we use.

[11:00] Dev Patel: And we can do a post-incident letter summarizing impact and mitigations. Some customers use that for internal reporting even if credits aren’t triggered.

[11:09] Hannah Wu: That would help.

[11:11] Miguel Santos: Technical question: why didn’t autoscale kick in earlier? We had a predictable ramp.

[11:17] Rafael Mendes: Two reasons. One: the autoscaler keys off a few signals—GPU utilization, queue depth, and request rate. The request rate ramped, but the queue depth didn’t cross threshold until prompts got longer. Two: we cap autoscale for Dedicated to avoid runaway. Your cap was set based on your previous peak. You exceeded it.

[11:40] Priya Nair: So we should rebaseline the cap.

[11:43] Rafael Mendes: Yes. And we can set a “burst window” schedule that temporarily increases headroom during your known peak. That’s something Fleet can do.

[11:53] Jordan Lee: That’d be ideal. Our peak is 9 to 11 Pacific weekdays.

[11:57] Rafael Mendes: Noted.

[12:00] Miguel Santos: Also, you mentioned OOM. Does that mean we need a max tokens guardrail?

[12:05] Rafael Mendes: Potentially. You’re already at max_new_tokens=512, but total context length increased. We can add a safeguard at the gateway to reject extreme prompt sizes with a clear error before it harms the cluster.

[12:20] Priya Nair: We’d prefer a 4xx with “prompt too large” rather than random 5xx.

[12:24] Rafael Mendes: Exactly.

[12:26] Rachel Kim: On the comms side, that also gives us cleaner language: “requests exceeded configured limits.”

[12:33] Jordan Lee: Another comms point: update cadence. We got updates at like minute 0 and then minute 25.

[12:41] Rachel Kim: Yeah, that’s not acceptable for Sev1. Our draft template is every 10–15 minutes during active mitigation, even if the update is “still mitigating, no ETA.” We’ll standardize that.

[12:54] Dev Patel: And we’ll keep a “what we know / what we don’t know” section so we don’t speculate.

[13:01] Priya Nair: +1.

[13:03] Miguel Santos: Can you share the metrics you used? We only see the client side.

[13:07] Rafael Mendes: We can share a redacted snapshot: saturation, queue depth, error codes breakdown. For Dedicated, we can also expose some of this in Console, but it’s ops-only right now.

[13:21] Rachel Kim: We’re working on what’s safe to expose to CS and to customers; right now we’ll do a manual share for this incident.

[13:29] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[13:31] Rafael Mendes: I also want to address the routing thrash comment. We have a workstream to instrument “fallback reasons” and routing decisions better, so we can distinguish capacity-driven routing from policy misconfig.

[13:45] Priya Nair: Yeah, because from the outside it’s just errors.

[13:48] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. Today it’s too ambiguous.

[13:51] Lauren Bishop: This ties to remediation SLAs too. If it’s a pure fleet shortage, fix path is headroom + provisioning. If it’s routing misbehavior, fix path is code/config. Different owners.

[14:04] Jordan Lee: Makes sense.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk preventative actions. Rafael, what are you committing to on Redwood side?

[14:11] Rafael Mendes: Four items. One: adjust your Dedicated autoscale cap and add scheduled burst headroom. Two: enforce consistent execution profile across your pool—no silent drift. Three: implement earlier warning alerts—when we detect approaching saturation for Dedicated, we proactively notify you in Slack. Four: add a runbook + internal checklist so the on-call knows the tradeoff between failing fast (429) vs tail latency.

[14:40] Priya Nair: And streaming disconnects? Any specific fix?

[14:44] Rafael Mendes: We suspect some disconnects were gateway timeouts. We can tune the upstream idle timeout for streaming in your Dedicated gateway and ensure we’re not killing worker processes under memory pressure. I don’t want to promise full elimination without validating. But we’ll investigate.

[15:05] Miguel Santos: Our SDK upgrade around that time—could that have contributed?

[15:10] Priya Nair: We did roll a new version, yes.

[15:13] Rafael Mendes: Possibly, but the correlated saturation signals make me think server-side overload was primary. Still, if the new SDK changed retry behavior, it could amplify load. If you can share retry/backoff settings, we’ll sanity check.

[15:31] Jordan Lee: We can send that.

[15:33] Rachel Kim: Great.

[15:35] Hannah Wu: On the SLA / remediation language—can we get something we can copy/paste internally? Like definition of Sev1, mitigation, etc.

[15:44] Lauren Bishop: Yes. I’ll send the one-pager. It basically says: Severity is based on customer impact; mitigation target depends on tier; Dedicated has tighter comms expectations; exceptions include cloud provider outages, but we try to mitigate regardless.

[16:03] Jordan Lee: We’d like to know what counts as “cloud provider outage.” Because that can feel like a loophole.

[16:10] Lauren Bishop: Totally. It’s not meant as a loophole. It’s mainly for credit calculations, not for our operational urgency. Operationally, we still mitigate. The carve-out is for when the underlying cloud region is broadly impaired and we can’t provision/route around it.

[16:27] Priya Nair: In this case, it wasn’t that.

[16:29] Rafael Mendes: Correct.

[16:31] Rachel Kim: Can we talk about escalation path? During the incident, you posted in shared Slack. Did you know who to ping?

[16:38] Priya Nair: We pinged our TAM alias and then ended up in the incident channel. It worked, but it was a little chaotic.

[16:45] Dev Patel: We’re standardizing: for Dedicated Sev1/Sev0, you’ll get a single comms thread in shared Slack with an owner, and a backup email summary. And the incident commander role on our side is clear.

[17:00] Jordan Lee: That helps.

[17:02] Miguel Santos: Also, if the mitigation is “more 429s,” we want to know that explicitly so we can adjust client behavior.

[17:09] Rafael Mendes: Great call. We’ll include “expected client behavior” in comms: whether to retry, backoff, or reduce concurrency.

[17:18] Rachel Kim: That’s part of the templates.

[17:21] Priya Nair: One more technical: did you do any region failover?

[17:25] Rafael Mendes: No region failover. For your Dedicated, you’re single-region today with warm spares, but not active-active. We did not move to another region.

[17:36] Jordan Lee: Is active-active possible?

[17:38] Lauren Bishop: It’s possible as an add-on architecture, but it’s more cost. Let’s not derail today, but we can do a separate design session.

[17:48] Jordan Lee: Okay.

[17:50] Rachel Kim: I’m looking at time—we have ~10 minutes. I want to make sure we captured your top asks: earlier warning, consistent comms cadence with safer language, clarity on mitigation vs fix, and confidence there was no model fallback. Anything else?

[18:06] Priya Nair: That’s it for me.

[18:07] Miguel Santos: Just make sure the “profile drift” thing gets fixed. That’s important.

[18:12] Jordan Lee: And headroom planning.

[18:14] Rachel Kim: Great.

[18:16] Rafael Mendes: For the record, our internal follow-up will include adding better labels for incident type so we don’t end up with generic “overload” every time. This one should be “Dedicated capacity saturation.”

[18:29] Priya Nair: Yep.

[18:31] Rachel Kim: Cool. Next steps recap: Rafael will send the PIR write-up with timeline/metrics and final classification by Friday. Lauren sends the Dedicated remediation SLA one-pager and how it ties to credits evaluation. Dev sends comms template snippet and cadence expectations. And Acme will send workload forecast and retry/backoff info.

[18:55] Hannah Wu: And a follow-up meeting for the burst window config?

[18:58] Rachel Kim: Yes, we’ll schedule a 30-minute follow-up in two weeks. We’ll include Fleet as needed.

[19:05] Jordan Lee: Works.

[19:06] Priya Nair: Thanks.

[19:07] Miguel Santos: Thank you.

[19:09] Rachel Kim: Thanks everyone. We’ll get those notes out.

(Transcript ends — remainder of recording contained small talk / scheduling that was not fully transcribed.)

preview: Working session to finalize incident taxonomy v1 edges and the owner mapping for disputed categories. Team aligned on keeping top-level types small (target ~12) with subtypes where needed. title: Aurora Health Systems x Redwood — technical deep dive: overload behavior + SLAs
preview: Aurora asked detailed questions about Redwood’s new hosted overload protections: what errors look like (429 vs 503), whether Retry-After is always present, how to avoid retry storms, and how noisy-neighbor isolation works across tenants and tiers. Redwood described circuit breaker layering (gateway admission + routing + runtime backpressure), tenant/tier-aware policies, stable overload error codes with correlation IDs, and optional graceful degradation (fallback model/region, feature reduction like max_tokens caps / streaming concurrency). Aurora emphasized healthcare reliability expectations and asked how overload-induced failures map to SLAs/SLO reporting. Redwood committed to share the overload error taxonomy doc + sample responses, confirm Retry-After behavior across routes, and propose a pilot plan and SLA language for Hosted vs Dedicated.
