title: Northbridge AI Platform - QBR follow-up (status page transparency + incident comms)
preview:
Fireflies.ai - Call Transcript

Title: Northbridge AI Platform - QBR follow-up (status page transparency + incident comms)
Date: 2025-11-14
Start time: 4:00 PM ET
Duration: 53 minutes
Meeting link: (internal) https://meet.google.com/xxx-xxxx-xxx

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim (Enterprise CSM), Jasmine Liu (CS Leadership), Rafael Mendes (SRE), Wei Chen (Docs/Customer-facing language)
- Northbridge AI Platform: Alyssa Tran (Platform Eng Lead), Ben Roemer (SRE Manager), Priya Nair (Security)

Auto-summary (generated)
Northbridge reiterated that the public status page is currently too coarse for their operations team to trust during incidents. They want (1) per-region component visibility (US-East vs EU-West especially) and (2) consistent, publishable post-incident summaries that can be shared internally and with their own customers. Redwood reviewed the in-flight “status page transparency” work: component taxonomy by product surface + region, standardized incident update templates, and a post-incident summary format with confidentiality guardrails. Redwood committed to sharing draft taxonomy and a sample summary, and to inviting Northbridge into a limited rollout/beta.

Topics
- Status page granularity: product surface vs region vs capability (API vs streaming vs embeddings)
- When to mark “Degraded performance” vs “Partial outage” (latency-only scenarios)
- Initial incident updates: timing expectations and approval flow
- Post-incident summaries: content, confidentiality, and timing
- Dedicated / Private deployments: what can be shown publicly vs customer-specific

Action items (captured)
- Rachel (Redwood): Send draft component taxonomy + expected region list for v1 (US-East, US-West, EU-West) and how streaming is represented. (No date stated)
- Rafael (Redwood): Provide example of signal-to-component mapping and how they prevent flapping. (No date stated)
- Wei (Redwood): Share post-incident summary template and 1 sanitized example. (No date stated)
- Alyssa (Northbridge): Reply with “must-have” components + what their on-call needs to see to act (including streaming). (No date stated)

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Hey everyone. Thanks for making the time. This is the QBR follow-up you asked for, specifically on status page transparency and incident comms. We pulled in Rafael from SRE and Wei from docs so we can get concrete.

[00:14] Alyssa Tran: Appreciate it. Yeah, we had a QBR slide on “trust in status page” and we wanted to get to specifics.

[00:22] Jasmine Liu: Totally. And thanks for being direct about it in the QBR. It helps prioritize.

[00:28] Ben Roemer: Cool. Just to frame it, we’re not trying to be difficult. It’s just that when something is broken at 2 a.m., the current status page doesn’t help us decide if it’s Redwood or our own app.

[00:41] Rachel Kim: Makes sense. Maybe start with what didn’t work last time.

[00:46] Ben Roemer: The Nov 7 thing—streaming was basically… it wasn’t down down, but we had elevated disconnects in us-east. The status page said “API operational,” and then later a generic “degraded” but it wasn’t scoped. Our leadership asked why we didn’t notice sooner, and I’m like, because your status page is green.

[01:08] Alyssa Tran: And our customer success team uses your status page as a source of truth when our customers ask “is your AI feature down.” So we need something we can point to.

[01:18] Priya Nair: Also from compliance, we need an artifact after the fact that is shareable. Like a post-incident report, but not a deep technical postmortem with internal details.

[01:31] Rafael Mendes: Yep. That’s aligned with what we’re building. The main change is we’re splitting components by product surface and region, and where it matters, by capability, like streaming vs non-streaming.

[01:45] Ben Roemer: So, concretely, you’d have “Hosted API - US East” and “Hosted API - EU West” kind of thing?

[01:52] Rafael Mendes: Close. The draft taxonomy is more like: Hosted API (Chat/Generation), Streaming, Embeddings, Reranking, Console. Then each of those can be per region. So “Streaming - US-East” can degrade without painting all of Hosted API.

[02:08] Alyssa Tran: That would solve like 70% of the issue. Because the streaming failure modes are different.

[02:15] Wei Chen: And the language we publish will avoid implying full outage if it’s isolated. But we also don’t want to hide the ball, right.

[02:23] Priya Nair: I want to pull on that thread. Are you going to show “partial region” issues? Like only one AZ, or only some accounts?

[02:33] Rafael Mendes: We’re trying not to expose customer-specific capacity or account-level stuff. The public page won’t say “Northbridge is affected.” But if a region is broadly impacted, we will show that component as degraded in that region.

[02:49] Ben Roemer: Okay, but what if it’s only a subset? That’s the hard part. Because we were affected and it seemed like not everyone was.

[02:58] Rafael Mendes: Yep, that’s where we’re adding better internal-to-public mapping rules. Sometimes it’s a particular route or model version. In those cases, we still may mark the component, but the update text will be careful: “Some requests may fail” versus “All requests failing.”

[03:15] Jasmine Liu: And we’re standardizing the updates so we don’t end up with vague language like “we’re looking into it” for 45 minutes.

[03:24] Alyssa Tran: Yeah. That’s our second request: time to first update. What are you targeting?

[03:31] Rachel Kim: Internally we’re pushing for first public update quickly, but we don’t want to over-promise on an SLA on comms. The goal we’ve been using is “within 15 minutes of confirming customer impact” for Sev1/Sev0. Rafael, do you want to caveat?

[03:46] Rafael Mendes: The nuance is: we don’t want to publish false positives or flip-flop the status. So we’re adding anti-flap logic and requiring at least one verification step. But yes, we’re measuring time to first update.

[04:03] Ben Roemer: I’m fine with verification, but right now it’s like… silence. And then we open a ticket and we get “we’re investigating.” That’s not great.

[04:13] Rachel Kim: Understood.

[04:15] Wei Chen: For the initial update, we’re using a template that basically says: what we’re seeing, scope (component + region), and what customers can do. Without root cause. So it’s not “we don’t know,” it’s “we see elevated errors for streaming in us-east; we’re investigating; next update in X minutes.”

[04:35] Alyssa Tran: Do you commit to the “next update in X minutes” thing? Because if you say 30 minutes and then it’s 2 hours, that also hurts trust.

[04:44] Jasmine Liu: We’re trying to make it realistic. Like 30–60 minutes depending on severity. And if we miss it, we’d post a quick “still investigating, no ETA yet.” It’s better than silence.

[05:00] Ben Roemer: Okay. Region scoping is still number one for us. We run us-east and eu-west primarily. If us-east is melting but eu-west is fine, we want to know.

[05:12] Rafael Mendes: That’s in scope. The region list for v1 will likely be US-East, US-West, EU-West. APAC later. We also want to avoid a giant page with 50 components, so we’re grouping visually.

[05:28] Priya Nair: Please don’t hide it in a dropdown though.

[05:31] Rachel Kim: (laughs) Noted.

[05:35] Alyssa Tran: Also, embeddings. We had a separate incident where embeddings were failing but chat was okay. Again, it showed as “API operational.”

[05:44] Rafael Mendes: That’s exactly why embeddings is becoming its own component. We also found a classification bug that mis-labeled some embedding errors as chat. We fixed that in instrumentation, but the bigger fix is the component split.

[06:03] Ben Roemer: Great.

[06:05] Rachel Kim: The third request you mentioned, Priya, was post-incident summaries.

[06:10] Priya Nair: Yes. What we need is: a summary we can send to our internal stakeholders and auditors if needed. Not a full RCA with proprietary details. But something that says: impact window, affected services, what mitigations were done, and what you’re doing to prevent it. And ideally, consistent format.

[06:29] Wei Chen: That’s exactly the template. Sections: “Customer impact,” “Timeline,” “Root cause category” (not necessarily the full technical root cause if that’s sensitive), “Mitigation,” “Prevention / follow-ups.”

[06:45] Alyssa Tran: Root cause category is fine. Like “networking issue,” “capacity,” “software regression.” You don’t have to name the exact internal service.

[06:54] Rafael Mendes: Right. Also we want to avoid saying “capacity” in a way that reveals customer-specific constraints. But we can say “infrastructure dependency degradation,” stuff like that.

[07:05] Ben Roemer: Timing. When do you publish those?

[07:08] Rachel Kim: We’re aligning on a policy. Internally we do postmortems, but the publishable summary should come out faster. The current proposal is within 5–7 business days for Sev1/Sev0 incidents, after review.

[07:23] Priya Nair: Who approves? That’s important for us.

[07:26] Jasmine Liu: In practice: Incident Commander owns the facts, Comms Lead drafts, and there’s a lightweight review for high-severity—SRE leadership and Support/CS signoff, sometimes legal if it’s sensitive.

[07:41] Priya Nair: Good. We don’t want something posted that later gets edited materially.

[07:47] Wei Chen: There may be minor edits for clarity, but we’re trying to avoid “changing the story.” The template also forces us to be explicit about uncertainty.

[07:57] Ben Roemer: On that: during the incident, can you say “cause unknown” explicitly? It’s okay.

[08:03] Wei Chen: Yes. We’ve been pushing for language like: “Root cause not yet confirmed. We will provide more details in a post-incident summary.” That avoids speculation.

[08:15] Alyssa Tran: Great.

[08:17] Rachel Kim: I want to check one thing: you also asked for “per-region component visibility.” Are you expecting that for Dedicated and Private too? Or just Hosted API?

[08:29] Alyssa Tran: We’re primarily on Hosted right now, but we’re evaluating Dedicated. For Dedicated, we’d want at least control plane visibility. But we understand you might not show customer-specific stuff publicly.

[08:40] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. Dedicated and Private can be tricky. Public status is for the shared platform. For Dedicated, we can provide customer-specific status via the Console, not necessarily public. But we’re also adding “Dedicated control plane” as a public-ish component if there’s a widespread issue.

[08:58] Ben Roemer: So, if our dedicated cluster is down but nobody else is, you wouldn’t post publicly.

[09:04] Rafael Mendes: Right. We’d communicate directly. But if there’s an outage in the Dedicated control plane that affects multiple dedicated customers, we can post that as a component.

[09:16] Priya Nair: That’s okay. We just need clarity in the contract and in the runbooks.

[09:21] Rachel Kim: Understood.

[09:24] Ben Roemer: Another detail: latency-only degradations. Sometimes things are slow, but not erroring. Do you mark that as degraded?

[09:33] Rafael Mendes: We’re finalizing criteria. Not every spike, but sustained SLO burn or p95/p99 latency crossing thresholds with customer impact, yes. The status would be “Degraded Performance.” We’re adding anti-flap so it doesn’t toggle.

[09:51] Alyssa Tran: We’d rather you mark it than not. If we see our requests take 20 seconds, it’s functionally broken for our users.

[09:58] Jasmine Liu: That’s consistent feedback. Support gets tickets like “it’s green but it’s unusable.”

[10:06] Rachel Kim: Ben, anything else on what you need to see on the page when you’re on-call?

[10:11] Ben Roemer: Two things: (1) Which regions, (2) which capability. For example “Streaming - US East.” And then the update should say if there’s a workaround. Like “disable streaming” or “retry with non-streaming.”

[10:27] Rafael Mendes: We can include mitigations like “fallback active” or “increased capacity” in plain language. We do have to be careful about advising changes that might break customer apps, but we can suggest safe mitigations.

[10:42] Alyssa Tran: Another one: you should show the incident history and link to the post-incident summary. Right now it’s hard to find.

[10:50] Wei Chen: That’s part of it. The incident page will link to the summary once published. Also we’re integrating links from the Console.

[11:00] Rachel Kim: Okay. Let’s spend the remaining time on what we can share and when.

[11:06] Jasmine Liu: We can’t share internal drafts broadly yet, but we can share the template and a sanitized example now. For the component taxonomy, we can share the proposed list and which ones will be in v1.

[11:19] Alyssa Tran: That’s fine. We don’t need your internal docs, just something we can validate.

[11:25] Ben Roemer: And if you have a beta, we’d like to be in it.

[11:28] Rachel Kim: Noted, we can include you in the limited rollout group.

[11:33] Priya Nair: One more question: will you ever include customer data in the post-incident summary? Like, “Customer X saw Y.”

[11:39] Wei Chen: No. We explicitly avoid that. It’s written to be safe for broad publication.

[11:46] Priya Nair: Great.

[11:49] Rachel Kim: I’m going to jump ahead a bit—Rafael, can you explain how you decide which component is impacted? Just so Ben has confidence this isn’t manual guessing.

[11:59] Rafael Mendes: Yeah. Internally we tag metrics by component_id, region, and capability. The gateway emits tags, and then our dashboards and alerts line up with the status components. So if streaming disconnects in us-east cross a threshold, the “Streaming - US-East” component is what triggers. Humans verify before posting.

[12:22] Ben Roemer: Good. So it’s not “someone remembers to click streaming.”

[12:26] Rafael Mendes: (laughs) That’s the idea. Also we log changes for audit.

[12:33] Alyssa Tran: Will your first update include numbers? Like “X% error rate”?

[12:38] Wei Chen: Usually we keep it qualitative, like “elevated errors,” unless the number is stable and meaningful. We found numbers can be misleading early on.

[12:49] Ben Roemer: Okay. As long as it’s scoped and timely.

[12:54] Rachel Kim: We’re at a good spot. Do you have any other asks?

[12:58] Alyssa Tran: Just a request: please don’t call everything “degraded.” There’s a difference between “some retries” and “it’s unusable.”

The status page said “API operational,” and then later a generic “degraded” but it wasn’t scoped. Our leadership asked why we didn’t notice sooner, and I’m like, because your status page is green.

[01:08] Alyssa Tran: And our customer success team uses your status page as a source of truth when our customers ask “is your AI feature down.” So we need something we can point to.

[01:18] Priya Nair: Also from compliance, we need an artifact after the fact that is shareable. Like a post-incident report, but not a deep technical postmortem with internal details.

[01:31] Rafael Mendes: Yep. That’s aligned with what we’re building. The main change is we’re splitting components by product surface and region, and where it matters, by capability, like streaming vs non-streaming.

[02:15] Wei Chen: And the language we publish will avoid implying full outage if it’s isolated. But we also don’t want to hide the ball, right.

[02:49] Ben Roemer: Okay, but what if it’s only a subset? That’s the hard part. Because we were affected and it seemed like not everyone was.

[02:58] Rafael Mendes: Yep, that’s where we’re adding better internal-to-public mapping rules. Sometimes it’s a particular route or model version. In those cases, we still may mark the component, but the update text will be careful: “Some requests may fail” versus “All requests failing.”

[05:12] Rafael Mendes: That’s in scope. The region list for v1 will likely be US-East, US-West, EU-West. APAC later. We also want to avoid a giant page with 50 components, so we’re grouping visually.

[06:10] Priya Nair: Yes. What we need is: a summary we can send to our internal stakeholders and auditors if needed. Not a full RCA with proprietary details. [06:29] Wei Chen: That’s exactly the template. Sections: “Customer impact,” “Timeline,” “Root cause category” (not necessarily the full technical root cause if that’s sensitive), “Mitigation,” “Prevention / follow-ups.”

[06:45] Alyssa Tran: Root cause category is fine. Like “networking issue,” “capacity,” “software regression.” You don’t have to name the exact internal service.

[06:54] Rafael Mendes: Right. Also we want to avoid saying “capacity” in a way that reveals customer-specific constraints. But we can say “infrastructure dependency degradation,” stuff like that.

[07:05] Ben Roemer: Timing. When do you publish those?

[07:08] Rachel Kim: We’re aligning on a policy. Internally we do postmortems, but the publishable summary should come out faster. The current proposal is within 5–7 business days for Sev1/Sev0 incidents, after review.

[07:23] Priya Nair: Who approves? That’s important for us.

[07:26] Jasmine Liu: In practice: Incident Commander owns the facts, Comms Lead drafts, and there’s a lightweight review for high-severity—SRE leadership and Support/CS signoff, sometimes legal if it’s sensitive.

[07:41] Priya Nair: Good. We don’t want something posted that later gets edited materially.

[07:47] Wei Chen: There may be minor edits for clarity, but we’re trying to avoid “changing the story.” The template also forces us to be explicit about uncertainty.

[07:57] Ben Roemer: On that: during the incident, can you say “cause unknown” explicitly? It’s okay.

[08:03] Wei Chen: Yes. We’ve been pushing for language like: “Root cause not yet confirmed. We will provide more details in a post-incident summary.” That avoids speculation.

[09:24] Ben Roemer: Another detail: latency-only degradations. Sometimes things are slow, but not erroring. Do you mark that as degraded?

[09:33] Rafael Mendes: We’re finalizing criteria. Not every spike, but sustained SLO burn or p95/p99 latency crossing thresholds with customer impact, yes. The status would be “Degraded Performance.” We’re adding anti-flap so it doesn’t toggle.

[10:27] Rafael Mendes: We can include mitigations like “fallback active” or “increased capacity” in plain language. We do have to be careful about advising changes that might break customer apps, but we can suggest safe mitigations.

[10:42] Alyssa Tran: Another one: you should show the incident history and link to the post-incident summary. Right now it’s hard to find.

[10:50] Wei Chen: That’s part of it. The incident page will link to the summary once published. Also we’re integrating links from the Console.

So if streaming disconnects in us-east cross a threshold, the “Streaming - US-East” component is what triggers. Humans verify before posting.

title: Private observability minimum pack - technical deep dive (SIEM + air gap + alerting)
preview:
Meeting: Private observability minimum pack - technical deep dive (SIEM + air gap + alerting)
Date/Time: Jun 12, 2025 4:03 PM PT
Duration: 57 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Markus Klein, Colin O'Donnell, Fatima Noor, Logan Wright, Rafael Mendes
- Harrington National Bank: Priya Desai (Platform Eng), Kevin Lau (SRE), Monica Reyes (SecOps/SIEM), Dmitri Volkov (Infra Architect), Alyssa Tran (GRC)

---
Summary (auto-generated)
Harrington reviewed requirements for a "minimum supported" observability bundle for Redwood Private deployments. Key needs: (1) log export to Splunk (HEC) with strong redaction and long retention on the SIEM side, (2) feasibility in air-gapped environments including offline upgrades and artifact signing, (3) alerting that aligns to SLO/error budget with low noise and clear runbooks, (4) bounded metrics cardinality and safe defaults for Prometheus/Grafana in customer clusters.

Topics (auto-generated)
- What ships in the minimum pack vs premium
- Metrics: Prometheus, naming, label cardinality constraints
- Dashboards: Grafana runtime + control plane + infra
- Alerting: severities, burn rate, paging policy alignment
- Logs: structured format, redaction, sinks (Splunk/Elastic/Loki/OTLP)
- Air-gapped packaging: images, manifests, checksums, upgrade/rollback

Action items (auto-generated)
- Redwood to send export integration examples and offline artifact list
- Harrington to confirm SIEM ingest preferences and SLO/paging policy

---
Transcript

[00:00] Markus Klein: Alright, cool. Folks are trickling in. I’m Markus, solutions engineering at Redwood. This is a technical deep dive specifically on the Private observability minimum pack. I’ve got Colin, Fatima, Logan, and Rafael with me.

[00:14] Priya Desai: Hi, I’m Priya, I run platform engineering for the AI platform side. Kevin is our SRE lead for that cluster and Monica is SecOps.

[00:22] Monica Reyes: Hey. Mainly here to make sure we don’t accidentally create a new data exfil path.

[00:26] (crosstalk)

[00:27] Markus Klein: Totally fair. The goal today is to understand what “supported minimum” needs to look like for you: dashboards, alerts, and log export, especially the air-gap constraints.

[00:39] Dmitri Volkov: Dmitri here. I’m the infra architect. Quick note: we have both connected VPC environments and true offline on-prem environments. Same product ideally.

[00:49] Fatima Noor: Great, that’s exactly the split we’re targeting: VPC private install, and on-prem air-gapped.

[01:00] Markus Klein: Before we jump in, quick framing. Minimum pack, as we define it, is baseline signals we can support and troubleshoot against: Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboards, curated alert rules, and a log pipeline that is safe by default. Not a full enterprise observability suite.

[01:18] Kevin Lau: “Safe by default” is doing a lot of work there. Like, what do you log and do you log prompts?

[01:24] Colin O'Donnell: Yeah, good question. Operational logs: think service health, request IDs, route name, model identifier in a constrained way, durations, status codes. We do not recommend logging raw prompts or raw completions in the default operational logs. If a customer turns on request/response sampling that’s an explicit opt-in and we’d still recommend redaction.

[01:45] Alyssa Tran: From GRC: we have a blanket restriction on customer PII. So we need defaults that assume PII exists in prompts.

[01:52] Logan Wright: Understood. For Private we treat payload as potentially sensitive. So default is structured logs without payloads. We can include hashed or truncated identifiers, and you can control the fields.

[02:05] Monica Reyes: When you say structured logs, what format? JSON? And are you expecting we run Loki, or can we just ship to Splunk?

[02:14] Logan Wright: JSON lines, yes. We can support a collector that forwards to multiple sinks. In practice we see: Splunk HEC, Elastic/OpenSearch, Loki, and in some cases an OpenTelemetry Collector pipeline.

[02:27] Monica Reyes: Splunk HEC is our standard.

[02:30] Colin O'Donnell: Great. We’ve done HEC a bunch. The big gotchas are TLS and token scoping, and making sure the collector buffers appropriately so it doesn’t drop under backpressure.

[02:42] Kevin Lau: Buffering is another way of saying “disk usage.” In on-prem, you can’t just balloon.

[02:48] Rafael Mendes: Right, and from SRE perspective, if the log forwarder goes unhealthy and starts OOMing, it’s worse than missing some debug logs. We tend to choose bounded buffers and clear alerting on drops.

[03:02] Markus Klein: Maybe we should structure this around three buckets: metrics/dashboards, alerts/paging, and logs/export + air-gap packaging. Priya, where do you want to start?

[03:12] Priya Desai: Start with logs/export and air gap. That’s where we’ve been burned.

[03:17] Markus Klein: Cool. Fatima, do you want to walk through what we typically ship in an air-gapped bundle?

[03:24] Fatima Noor: Sure. For air-gapped: all container images need to be mirrored into your private registry. Beyond images, we also need to package the Grafana dashboard JSONs, Prometheus rule files, and any default collector configs. The key is those “non-image” artifacts still need versioning and checksums.

[03:44] Dmitri Volkov: Do you sign them?

[03:46] Fatima Noor: We can provide checksums and a manifest. Signing depends on your verification pipeline; some customers want cosign for images plus a signed manifest for artifacts. We can align there, but I don’t want to promise a specific mechanism without scoping.

[04:04] Alyssa Tran: For on-prem, we require an SBOM and signature verification. If the dashboards are “just JSON files,” they still need to be controlled.

[04:13] Fatima Noor: Yep. The pattern we’ve used: bundle manifest listing image digests, dashboard/rule file hashes, and installer verifies at deploy time.

[04:25] Kevin Lau: And upgrades? Like, if you change metric names, our dashboards go blank.

[04:30] Rafael Mendes: That is a real issue. Minimum pack is aiming for upgrade-safe behavior. Either backwards compatible metrics, or the installer does a migration step, and we document breaking changes.

[04:42] Priya Desai: When you say “installer,” is this Helm charts or a separate installer binary?

[04:48] Fatima Noor: Both surfaces. A lot of customers use Helm directly, but for on-prem we also support an installer flow that orchestrates preflight checks and offline bundle handling.

[05:01] Monica Reyes: For Splunk HEC, do you support outbound only? We don’t allow inbound connectivity to cluster components.

[05:08] Colin O'Donnell: Outbound only is typical. Collector posts to HEC endpoint. For air-gapped, sometimes Splunk is internal, still reachable, but no internet. If even that is blocked, we’d need an internal forwarder in your environment.

[05:24] Dmitri Volkov: In the strictest zone, we can reach Splunk but through a proxy and with mTLS.

[05:30] Colin O'Donnell: Great. We’d need to model: proxy env vars, TLS settings, and cert distribution. The config can support client certs; it’s mostly about how you want to mount secrets.

[05:45] Monica Reyes: Also, field requirements: we need correlation across systems. request_id is required. user id can be present but must be hashed.

[05:54] Logan Wright: request_id we can do. For user/tenant, we typically include a stable hash, not the raw ID. Model name is tricky because it can explode cardinality; for logs it’s less of a metrics problem but still can be sensitive.

[06:09] Priya Desai: For metrics, yeah, we’ve seen “label cardinality storms.” How are you handling that?

[06:14] Logan Wright: We cap or remove high-cardinality labels in Private by default. Example: instead of per-tenant metrics, we give per-service and per-route aggregates, and limited model grouping. Anything per-tenant becomes an opt-in with guardrails.

[06:29] Kevin Lau: Is there a knob? We might want per-model for a short window.

[06:33] Logan Wright: There can be configuration toggles, yes, but we’ll be explicit what’s supported. Minimum pack wants to prevent Prometheus from falling over.

[06:45] Markus Klein: Monica, on the SIEM side, what retention do you need?

[06:49] Monica Reyes: We keep 365 days in Splunk for security events, but not necessarily for all operational logs. We’d likely split indexes.

[06:57] Colin O'Donnell: That’s common. Minimum pack defaults for in-cluster retention are shorter, like days not months. We rely on export for long retention.

[07:08] Alyssa Tran: And audit logs? Are they separate?

[07:12] Markus Klein: Redwood has audit logs for control-plane actions—who changed routing policy, who deployed a model version, etc. Operational logs are separate. For Private, we want clarity so you can ship audit logs to security retention without dragging in everything.

[07:30] Monica Reyes: If audit logs are “control plane,” does that include API gateway auth events?

[07:35] Colin O'Donnell: Typically, yes: auth failures, permission denies, config changes. But we have to be careful about what user attributes are included.

[07:47] Priya Desai: OK. Let’s talk dashboards/metrics quickly. What do you actually ship? We’re not looking for 200 dashboards.

[07:56] Markus Klein: Right, minimum pack is “curated.” Think a handful: runtime overview, control plane health, and infra basics.

[08:05] Rafael Mendes: Plus GPU fleet panel: utilization, memory, OOM kills, throttling, and queue/batching metrics. The “golden signals” for inference.

[08:15] Kevin Lau: Golden signals meaning latency, traffic, errors, saturation.

[08:18] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. And for LLM specifically: tokens/sec, queue depth, batch size, KV cache hit rate if exposed, and GPU memory headroom.

[08:31] Priya Desai: Are you using OpenTelemetry for traces?

[08:35] Logan Wright: In v1 minimum pack, traces are “where feasible.” Many private customers don’t want always-on tracing. We can support OTLP export but usually start with metrics+logs.

[08:48] Dmitri Volkov: Our org has an enterprise APM tool, Dynatrace. But it doesn’t always play well in Kubernetes without agents.

[08:56] Markus Klein: We’ve seen that. The minimum pack is not trying to replace your APM. It’s basically: give you something that works in any cluster, even offline, and gives Redwood Support a shared vocabulary.

[09:10] Kevin Lau: Alerting expectations: we don’t want a firehose. We want page-worthy alerts only. Others can go to ticket.

[09:17] Rafael Mendes: Same philosophy on our side. We usually propose severity mapping like Sev1 page: service down or severe error budget burn; Sev2 page or urgent ticket: approaching burn; Sev3 ticket: capacity warnings.

[09:33] Kevin Lau: We do SLO-based paging. Like burn rate alerting.

[09:36] Rafael Mendes: Great. We can align: multi-window burn rates for availability and p95 latency. For inference, we also include GPU OOM / crashloop if it impacts availability.

[09:51] Priya Desai: What’s your default SLO? If you bake in 99.9 vs 99.5 it changes alerting.

[09:57] Markus Klein: For Private we don’t assume a single SLO. The minimum pack provides recommended thresholds and you tune to your target. But we can offer a “starter profile.”

[10:09] Monica Reyes: Is there anything that alerts on “correctness”?

[10:14] Markus Klein: Not in the strict ML eval sense in minimum pack. We can alert on obvious correctness-adjacent signals: increased 5xx, timeouts, model load failures. Full quality regression monitoring is more premium / console-oriented.

[10:28] Kevin Lau: That’s fine. We have our own eval harness.

[10:33] (audio glitch)

[10:35] Markus Klein: Sorry, I think someone cut out. Kevin you’re back.

[10:38] Kevin Lau: Yeah. So, Prometheus and Grafana: do you assume we run them in-cluster?

[10:44] Logan Wright: Minimum pack assumes there is a Prometheus-compatible scraper and Grafana-compatible dashboard renderer available. We can ship a reference stack in the charts, but many customers plug into their existing Prometheus.

[10:59] Dmitri Volkov: Our connected VPC has a centralized Prometheus. On-prem, we usually have a local Prometheus because there’s no cross-zone traffic.

[11:07] Logan Wright: That’s common. We just need to document scrape config, TLS, and RBAC permissions.

[11:16] Priya Desai: NetworkPolicies: we lock down namespaces. We often break scrapes by accident.

[11:22] Rafael Mendes: We’ve seen incidents exactly like that. So one thing we’re adding is installer preflight checks: verify scrape endpoints are reachable, verify Grafana datasource auth works, and warn about NetworkPolicy rules.

[11:37] Kevin Lau: Preflight checks that block install, or just warnings?

[11:41] Fatima Noor: Both. Some are hard-blocking: missing storage class, no PVC provisioning, can’t pull images from your registry, etc. Some are warnings: scrape reachability if you chose “external Prometheus” but didn’t configure.

[11:58] Monica Reyes: On logs: are you deploying a collector like Fluent Bit?

[12:03] Logan Wright: Typically a lightweight collector, yes. Some customers prefer Vector. We’re trying to standardize on one supported default for the minimum pack.

[12:13] Colin O'Donnell: Regardless of collector, Splunk HEC config patterns are similar: endpoint, token, index/sourcetype mapping, and TLS settings.

[12:25] Monica Reyes: We need sourcetype separation: control plane, runtime, gateway.

[12:29] Logan Wright: That’s doable. The structured logs include service name and component; collector can route to different sourcetypes.

[12:39] Priya Desai: Do you include Kubernetes metadata? Namespace, pod, node.

[12:43] Logan Wright: Yes, but again bounded. pod name can be high churn but not cardinality poison for logs. For metrics, we avoid putting pod labels everywhere.

[12:55] Kevin Lau: I’m going to ask a dumb question: do you support Datadog instead? We already pay for it in VPC.

[13:02] Markus Klein: Not dumb. We don’t depend on Datadog for minimum pack. You can certainly ship logs/metrics there, but the “supported baseline” artifacts we ship are Prometheus and Grafana oriented.

[13:16] Priya Desai: That’s acceptable. We can translate. It’s mostly that we need something that works in on-prem.

[13:23] Dmitri Volkov: Exactly. On-prem won’t have Datadog.

[13:28] Markus Klein: Let’s get specific about air gap. Dmitri, what is “air gap” for you? No outbound to internet, but internal network exists?

[13:36] Dmitri Volkov: Correct. No outbound. Internal DNS, internal PKI. We have an artifact repository, a container registry, and Splunk.

[13:45] Fatima Noor: Perfect. Then our bundle needs to be importable into your artifact repo. We’ll provide a manifest of all images, plus dashboards/rules/configs. Upgrades: you import a new bundle, run installer upgrade, it swaps chart versions.

[14:02] Kevin Lau: Do you require Helm to fetch charts from the internet?

[14:06] Fatima Noor: No, we support packaging the charts. Either you host the chart repo internally or we ship chart archives as part of the bundle.

[14:15] Alyssa Tran: And you can show provenance? Like, these artifacts came from Redwood.

[14:20] Fatima Noor: We can provide checksums and signatures. We’ll follow up on your required scheme.

[14:28] Monica Reyes: Logging redaction: what does that mean concretely? Regex? allowlist?

[14:34] Colin O'Donnell: Prefer allowlist of fields that are safe. For example: request_id, status, latency_ms, route, model_group, error_class. If you need to include something sensitive, we hash it.

[14:50] Monica Reyes: We also need to ensure secrets don’t leak in error messages. Like if a downstream returns an auth header.

[14:56] Logan Wright: Yep. Part of our “safe defaults” is not logging headers, and scrubbing known patterns. But we won’t claim it’s perfect; we should treat application logs as potentially sensitive and rely on both prevention and export controls.

[15:13] Priya Desai: Are there any logs from the runtime that include token content?

[15:17] Logan Wright: Not by default. Some debug mode could. Minimum pack would keep debug mode off.

[15:24] Kevin Lau: On metrics: do you have histograms for latency? We use exemplars sometimes.

[15:29] Logan Wright: Yes, histograms for request latency. Exemplars are possible, but in private environments it depends on Prometheus version and configuration.

[15:41] Rafael Mendes: And for alerting, we typically use recording rules to compute p95/p99 from histograms. That keeps dashboards fast.

[15:52] Priya Desai: OK. What about GPU metrics? NVIDIA DCGM exporter?

[15:57] Rafael Mendes: Usually yes. Or node exporter + GPU exporter. Minimum pack can integrate, but customers sometimes already have it.

[16:07] Dmitri Volkov: We have DCGM already.

[16:10] Markus Klein: Great, then our dashboards can just read those metrics.

[16:17] Monica Reyes: Switching back: we need “alert annotations” to include what to do. Not just “GPU high.”

[16:24] Rafael Mendes: Totally. We put runbook links and “first steps” in the alert. Like: check scrape health, check crashloops, check queue depth, check GPU memory.

[16:36] Kevin Lau: Do you page on “GPU utilization high”? Because that’s normal.

[16:40] Rafael Mendes: No. High utilization alone is not page-worthy. We page on symptoms: sustained queue growth causing latency SLO burn, OOM kills, or saturation causing elevated errors/timeouts.

[16:56] Priya Desai: Good.

[16:57] (brief silence)

[17:00] Markus Klein: Can we talk about your paging policy? Like what’s a Sev1 for you.

[17:05] Kevin Lau: Sev1 is customer impact: API down, or >5% errors, or p95 latency doubled for 10-15 minutes. We use burn rate alerts for error budget.

[17:17] Rafael Mendes: That maps well. We’ll propose: availability burn alerts + latency burn alerts, plus a small set of infrastructure failure alerts (Prometheus not scraping, Grafana datasource broken) but those might be ticket, not page.

[17:36] Priya Desai: “Prometheus not scraping” should page if it blinds us during an incident.

[17:41] Rafael Mendes: Fair. Some customers page on loss of observability for critical services.

[17:47] Monica Reyes: For security, we need an alert when audit logs stop flowing to Splunk. That’s a control.

[17:55] Colin O'Donnell: Good point. Collector can expose “dropped logs” or “failed deliveries” metrics and we can alert.

[18:03] Logan Wright: Also, you can add a heartbeat event. Like every minute a known log line is emitted and you alert if it’s missing downstream.

[18:14] Monica Reyes: We do that for some systems.

[18:16] Markus Klein: Great. That’s an integration point we can document.

[18:21] Priya Desai: Another requirement: we don’t want to have to customize dashboards heavily. We need them out-of-the-box.

[18:27] Markus Klein: That’s the intention. The pack ships a curated dashboard set. You can clone them, but we support the defaults.

[18:36] Dmitri Volkov: What happens if we rename namespaces or use different service names?

[18:41] Logan Wright: We avoid hardcoding names; we rely on labels like job/service. But we do need a consistent label mapping. Part of the pack is standardizing that.

[18:55] Kevin Lau: So you’re effectively defining metric names and label keys.

[18:58] Logan Wright: Yes, for Redwood components. And we provide compatibility notes.

[19:06] Priya Desai: Are you planning to include model registry integration signals? Like “model download failed.”

[19:12] Markus Klein: Yes, that’s in-scope. Control plane and registry integration points: failed pulls, auth errors, latency to registry.

[19:24] Kevin Lau: That’s huge for us. We saw timeouts pulling large weights.

[19:29] Rafael Mendes: Then we want panels for: model load duration, download throughput, retry counts, and error classes. Plus alerts if model load fails repeatedly.

[19:44] Monica Reyes: Careful with logging registry URLs. Some are internal.

[19:48] Colin O'Donnell: We can log hostname category or a redacted value.

[19:55] Markus Klein: Quick check: do you require FedRAMP-like controls? Or just internal policy.

[20:01] Alyssa Tran: Not FedRAMP, but we align to SOC2-ish and internal banking controls. Main thing: retention, access control, and change management.

[20:10] Priya Desai: And “no internet.”

[20:12] Markus Klein: Got it.

[20:14] Kevin Lau: On Grafana: do you ship datasources too? Or do we configure.

[20:19] Logan Wright: We can ship a default datasource if you deploy Grafana as part of the pack. If you use existing Grafana, you import dashboards.

[20:30] Dmitri Volkov: We have enterprise Grafana.

[20:33] Markus Klein: Then we likely just provide dashboard JSONs and a mapping doc: which datasource variable to set.

[20:41] Monica Reyes: Is there any scenario where Redwood Support needs access to our Grafana?

[20:46] Markus Klein: Not required. Usually customers share screenshots or export panel data. For some engagements we do a temporary support access with your approval.

[20:56] Priya Desai: We can’t allow that in prod.

[20:58] Markus Klein: Totally fine.

[21:02] (crosstalk)

[21:03] Kevin Lau: Let’s talk about what you consider “minimum supported.” Like if we disable half the alerts, are we unsupported?

[21:10] Rafael Mendes: You can tune thresholds and disable some alerts, but we’ll define a baseline set that, if completely removed, makes troubleshooting harder. In other words: we support the pack as shipped.

[21:25] Priya Desai: That’s reasonable.

[21:27] Monica Reyes: For logs, can we choose only audit logs to go to Splunk and keep operational logs local?

[21:34] Logan Wright: Yes, you can route different streams differently. But for incident response, operational logs in Splunk are very useful.

[21:44] Monica Reyes: Understood. We might split indexes and retention.

[21:49] Colin O'Donnell: That’s the usual approach.

[21:54] Markus Klein: We’re at ~22 minutes. Let me ask: what would make this a “yes” for you on observability? Like what are the must-haves.

[22:02] Priya Desai: Must-haves: out-of-box dashboards for runtime and control plane; basic alerting with SLO alignment; ability to export logs to Splunk in both VPC and on-prem; and an air-gap bundle with all artifacts, versioned.

[22:19] Kevin Lau: Plus bounded cardinality and no surprise scrape overload.

[22:23] Monica Reyes: And: clear redaction defaults, no prompts in logs, and ability to prove what’s being logged.

[22:30] Markus Klein: Great. That aligns to what we’re building.

[22:34] Dmitri Volkov: One more: we need to know resource footprint. Prometheus can be heavy.

[22:40] Rafael Mendes: Yes, sizing guidance is part of it. Usually we propose a baseline PVC size and retention; you adjust based on rate.

[22:50] Kevin Lau: If we have 2k RPS, what’s the Prometheus storage?

[22:55] Rafael Mendes: Hard to answer without sample rate and metrics set, but rule of thumb: keep metrics minimal, scrape every 15-30 seconds, and store 7-14 days locally. Export long-term elsewhere.

[23:10] Priya Desai: OK.

[23:12] Markus Klein: Logan, can you speak to what sinks you consider “supported” in v1? Monica asked OTLP.

[23:19] Logan Wright: Yeah. For minimum pack, I’d say: Splunk HEC and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch are the primary export targets we’ll document and test. Loki is also common if you’re Grafana-first. OTLP is on the roadmap; some customers want it, but we need to be precise: OTLP logs vs traces, and what’s actually validated in air-gapped.

[23:43] Monica Reyes: That’s fine. Splunk first.

[23:46] Markus Klein: Great.

[23:48] Kevin Lau: For alerting, do you ship PagerDuty integration?

[23:52] Rafael Mendes: We ship Prometheus alert rules. Routing to PagerDuty is via Alertmanager config, which you already own. We can provide an example.

[24:04] Kevin Lau: OK.

[24:06] Priya Desai: On-prem upgrades: do you support rollback?

[24:10] Fatima Noor: Yes, but with caveats. If we change dashboards and rules, rollback should still function. The tricky part is metric schema changes. We aim for backward compatibility or explicit migrations.

[24:24] Dmitri Volkov: We need a statement of compatibility windows.

[24:28] Markus Klein: We can provide that in writing. Like: “metrics are stable across minor versions” or similar.

[24:35] Alyssa Tran: Also, do you keep logs of admin actions in the installer itself? That’s a compliance requirement.

[24:42] Fatima Noor: Installer can emit audit events: who ran upgrade, what version, what manifest hash. Those can go to your SIEM.

[24:53] Monica Reyes: Great.

[24:55] Kevin Lau: I want to ask about “noise.” How do you validate you’re not shipping noisy alerts?

[25:01] Rafael Mendes: We typically run alert-noise review in staging: replay load, inject failures, see what fires. Then we pick conservative defaults. Some alerts will be disabled by default if they’re environment-specific.

[25:16] Priya Desai: Which ones would be disabled by default?

[25:19] Rafael Mendes: Things like “disk almost full” depends on your PVC sizing. Or “GPU utilization high” which isn’t actionable. But “error budget burn” should be on.

[25:32] Kevin Lau: Makes sense.

[25:34] Markus Klein: Monica, on SIEM, do you require CEF or syslog?

[25:39] Monica Reyes: We prefer HEC JSON. CEF is legacy.

[25:43] Colin O'Donnell: Great. We’ll stick to JSON. We can map fields to your CIM expectations.

[25:52] Monica Reyes: Also: multi-line logs are a pain.

[25:55] Logan Wright: Agree. We try to keep one JSON per event. Stack traces might be truncated or structured as arrays, but collector compatibility varies.

[26:06] Priya Desai: Another question: do you have per-model latency? That’s important for us.

[26:12] Logan Wright: We can have per-model “group” metrics, but full per-model label can be high cardinality. We might offer “top N models” or a controlled label set.

[26:26] Kevin Lau: We only have like 6 models.

[26:29] Logan Wright: In that case it’s probably fine. But we can’t assume that across customers.

[26:35] Markus Klein: So we can document an opt-in: enable per-model label if model count < X.

[26:42] Kevin Lau: Exactly.

[26:44] Dmitri Volkov: What about tenant? We have multi-tenant internal apps.

[26:48] Logan Wright: Tenant labels get dangerous quickly. For Private, we generally avoid tenant labels in metrics. For logs, you can include tenant hash.

[26:58] Monica Reyes: That’s acceptable for SIEM.

[27:01] Markus Klein: We’re coming up on 30 minutes. Maybe we do a quick walkthrough of what we think the dashboard set is, and you tell us if anything is missing.

[27:09] Priya Desai: Sure.

[27:10] Markus Klein: Dashboard 1: Runtime Overview. Panels: request rate, error rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, token throughput, queue depth, batch size, GPU util/mem, OOM count, restarts.

[27:27] Kevin Lau: Include saturation like pending requests.

[27:30] Markus Klein: Yep, queue/backpressure.

[27:33] Markus Klein: Dashboard 2: Control Plane Health. API gateway status, auth/RBAC integration points, policy/routing service health, model registry connectivity and errors.

[27:47] Priya Desai: Include config rollouts.

[27:49] Markus Klein: Yes: deployment controller status, reconcile loops, upgrade controller errors.

[27:56] Markus Klein: Dashboard 3: Infra baseline. Prometheus scrape health, alertmanager, grafana datasource, node resource pressure, persistent volume usage.

[28:10] Dmitri Volkov: That’s good.

[28:12] Monica Reyes: Anything around “log forwarder health”? Like if the collector is failing.

[28:17] Logan Wright: That’s part of infra: forwarder CPU/memory, queue depth, dropped events, retries.

[28:26] Monica Reyes: Good.

[28:28] Markus Klein: Alerts: Availability SLO burn, latency SLO burn, elevated 5xx/timeouts, crashloop for runtime pods, GPU OOM/repeated OOM, queue depth sustained growth, model load failures, scrape failures, and log export failure/drops.

[28:52] Kevin Lau: That covers most.

[28:54] Priya Desai: For model load failures: page or ticket?

[28:58] Rafael Mendes: Depends. If it affects serving capacity for an active model, page. If it’s a prefetch for a canary, ticket.

[29:07] Priya Desai: OK.

[29:10] Alyssa Tran: Can you make sure alerts don’t include sensitive content in annotations? Like a model name might be sensitive.

[29:17] Rafael Mendes: Good call. We can use sanitized identifiers in alert text and rely on internal dashboards for details.

[29:26] Monica Reyes: On Splunk, we can handle sensitive data if it’s in the right index with ACLs. But prefer not.

[29:32] Colin O'Donnell: We’ll take the conservative approach.

[29:36] Markus Klein: Air-gap again: Monica, do you allow pulling images from your internal registry only.

[29:41] Monica Reyes: Yes. No Docker Hub.

[29:44] Fatima Noor: Then the bundle provides all image digests; you mirror to registry; we configure imagePullSecrets.

[29:53] Dmitri Volkov: Do you have any external dependencies like helm chart pulling plugin stuff?

[29:58] Fatima Noor: No external dependencies at install time.

[30:01] Kevin Lau: OK.

[30:03] Markus Klein: I want to address one thing: how Redwood Support will troubleshoot in Private. We need consistent metric names and dashboards so we can ask for specific panels. That’s why we’re standardizing.

[30:16] Priya Desai: That’s fine.

[30:18] Monica Reyes: As long as you’re not requiring direct access.

[30:21] Markus Klein: Not required.

[30:24] Kevin Lau: What about “data plane vs control plane.” Are logs separate namespaces?

[30:29] Logan Wright: Often yes, separate deployments/namespaces. Logs include component field so you can route.

[30:38] Monica Reyes: We’ll want separate sourcetypes.

[30:41] Colin O'Donnell: Yep.

[30:44] Dmitri Volkov: One concern: some vendors ship “metrics with customer IDs” and it becomes a privacy issue. You’re saying you avoid that.

[30:52] Logan Wright: Correct. No raw tenant IDs in metrics by default.

[30:57] Priya Desai: OK.

[31:00] Markus Klein: Let’s open questions. What have we not covered that you need for a decision?

[31:06] Monica Reyes: Two questions. One: can we prove prompts are not logged. Two: can we disable debug logging at compile-time, not just config.

[31:15] Logan Wright: On proving: we can document log schema and provide a sample of log lines. On disabling debug: yes, we can ensure debug-level logs are off by default and require explicit configuration to enable more verbose logs.

[31:31] Colin O'Donnell: Also we can provide guidance on auditing: grep patterns, and Splunk queries that detect payload-like fields.

[31:40] Monica Reyes: That would help.

[31:42] Kevin Lau: My question: can you support external Prometheus with our own scrape intervals and TLS? Some vendors hardcode.

[31:49] Logan Wright: Yes. We expose metrics endpoints; you scrape however you want. We may ship a ServiceMonitor for convenience if you use Prometheus Operator.

[32:02] Priya Desai: OK.

[32:05] Dmitri Volkov: Are you requiring egress to GitHub for dashboards?

[32:09] Fatima Noor: No.

[32:11] Markus Klein: Another question: you mentioned Dynatrace. If later you want Dynatrace integration, that’s separate scope. Minimum pack is meant to be universal.

[32:23] Priya Desai: Understood.

[32:26] Kevin Lau: For thresholds, do you have recommended values? Like p95 > X.

[32:31] Rafael Mendes: We prefer SLO-based rather than absolute. But we can provide starter SLOs. For example: availability 99.9, latency p95 < 2s for typical prompts. But your workload varies.

[32:45] Kevin Lau: Yeah, ours is weird because we do long context.

[32:48] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. Better to alert on deviations and burn rate.

[32:54] Markus Klein: Let’s talk about traces briefly. Is it a blocker if v1 doesn’t include full tracing?

[33:00] Priya Desai: Not a blocker. Logs+metrics are enough.

[33:03] Kevin Lau: Same.

[33:04] Monica Reyes: As long as we can correlate via request_id.

[33:08] Logan Wright: request_id will be consistent across logs and metrics labels where possible (careful with cardinality), and definitely in logs.

[33:18] Monica Reyes: OK.

[33:20] Markus Klein: Rafael, any “day 2” gotchas we should warn them about? From past private installs.

[33:26] Rafael Mendes: Biggest ones: Grafana dashboards import but datasource name mismatch so everything is blank. Prometheus scrape fails due to NetworkPolicy. Alertmanager routes not configured so alerts don’t go anywhere. And log forwarder backpressure causing drops.

[33:47] Kevin Lau: Yep, seen all of those.

[33:50] Priya Desai: If you have a runbook that says “if dashboards blank, check X,” that’s helpful.

[33:56] Markus Klein: We’ll include that.

[34:00] Dmitri Volkov: How do you validate this in your CI? Like do you have smoke tests.

[34:06] Fatima Noor: We have a smoke-test harness that deploys a minimal cluster and checks that scrapes are happening, dashboards render, and alerts fire under a few injected failures.

[34:21] Dmitri Volkov: Good. We’ll want to see results or at least know it exists.

[34:26] Markus Klein: We can share a high-level validation plan.

[34:31] Monica Reyes: One last SIEM thing: Splunk HEC 401 is common. Do you have troubleshooting steps.

[34:37] Colin O'Donnell: Yes. Usually token mis-scope, wrong endpoint path (/services/collector/event), or TLS mismatch. We’ll provide a checklist.

[34:50] Monica Reyes: Great.

[34:52] Markus Klein: Alright, let’s move to recap and next steps.

[34:56] Markus Klein: What I heard: you need Splunk HEC export, safe structured logging with no payloads, air-gap bundles with all artifacts and provenance, Prometheus/Grafana dashboards that are curated and work out-of-box, and alerting aligned to SLO burn rates with low noise and embedded runbooks.

[35:16] Priya Desai: Yes.

[35:18] Kevin Lau: Yes.

[35:19] Monica Reyes: Yep.

[35:21] Markus Klein: Next steps from Redwood: we’ll send the dashboard catalog and alert set, the export integration matrix, and draft air-gap artifact list. Colin will follow with Splunk HEC config example. Fatima will outline offline packaging and verification assumptions. Logan will confirm what sinks are supported in the initial minimum pack.

[35:45] Markus Klein: Next steps from Harrington: Monica, confirm SIEM ingest preferences and required fields. Kevin, share SLO/paging policy and what’s page-worthy. Priya, confirm whether we can run a small in-cluster Prometheus in on-prem or if it must integrate with your central tools.

[36:08] Priya Desai: Yep, we can do that.

[36:10] Kevin Lau: Sure.

[36:11] Monica Reyes: Yep.

[36:13] Markus Klein: Great. We’ll schedule a follow-up, probably 45 minutes, with your SecOps and Platform folks to review redaction and retention. Sound good?

[36:22] Priya Desai: Works.

[36:24] Markus Klein: Cool. Thanks everyone.

[36:27] (meeting ends in recording shortly after; minor tail)

Transcript

[00:00] Markus Klein: Alright, cool. Folks are trickling in. I’m Markus, solutions engineering at Redwood. This is a technical deep dive specifically on the Private observability minimum pack. I’ve got Colin, Fatima, Logan, and Rafael with me.

[00:14] Priya Desai: Hi, I’m Priya, I run platform engineering for the AI platform side. Kevin is our SRE lead for that cluster and Monica is SecOps.

[00:22] Monica Reyes: Hey. Mainly here to make sure we don’t accidentally create a new data exfil path.

[00:26] (crosstalk)

[00:27] Markus Klein: Totally fair. The goal today is to understand what “supported minimum” needs to look like for you: dashboards, alerts, and log export, especially the air-gap constraints.

[00:39] Dmitri Volkov: Dmitri here. I’m the infra architect. Quick note: we have both connected VPC environments and true offline on-prem environments. Same product ideally.

[00:49] Fatima Noor: Great, that’s exactly the split we’re targeting: VPC private install, and on-prem air-gapped.

[01:00] Markus Klein: Before we jump in, quick framing. Minimum pack, as we define it, is baseline signals we can support and troubleshoot against: Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboards, curated alert rules, and a log pipeline that is safe by default. Not a full enterprise observability suite.

[01:18] Kevin Lau: “Safe by default” is doing a lot of work there. Like, what do you log and do you log prompts?

[32:48] Rafael Mendes: Exactly. Better to alert on deviations and burn rate.

[32:54] Markus Klein: Let’s talk about traces briefly. Is it a blocker if v1 doesn’t include full tracing?

[33:00] Priya Desai: Not a blocker. Logs+metrics are enough.

[33:03] Kevin Lau: Same.

[33:04] Monica Reyes: As long as we can correlate via request_id.

[33:08] Logan Wright: request_id will be consistent across logs and metrics labels where possible (careful with cardinality), and definitely in logs.

[33:18] Monica Reyes: OK.

[33:20] Markus Klein: Rafael, any “day 2” gotchas we should warn them about? From past private installs.

[33:26] Rafael Mendes: Biggest ones: Grafana dashboards import but datasource name mismatch so everything is blank. Prometheus scrape fails due to NetworkPolicy. Alertmanager routes not configured so alerts don’t go anywhere. And log forwarder backpressure causing drops.

[33:47] Kevin Lau: Yep, seen all of those.

[33:50] Priya Desai: If you have a runbook that says “if dashboards blank, check X,” that’s helpful.

[33:56] Markus Klein: We’ll include that.

[34:00] Dmitri Volkov: How do you validate this in your CI? Like do you have smoke tests.

[34:06] Fatima Noor: We have a smoke-test harness that deploys a minimal cluster and checks that scrapes are happening, dashboards render, and alerts fire under a few injected failures.

[34:21] Dmitri Volkov: Good. We’ll want to see results or at least know it exists.

[34:26] Markus Klein: We can share a high-level validation plan.

[34:31] Monica Reyes: One last SIEM thing: Splunk HEC 401 is common. Do you have troubleshooting steps.

[34:37] Colin O'Donnell: Yes. Usually token mis-scope, wrong endpoint path (/services/collector/event), or TLS mismatch. We’ll provide a checklist.

[34:50] Monica Reyes: Great.

[34:52] Markus Klein: Alright, let’s move to recap and next steps.

[34:56] Markus Klein: What I heard: you need Splunk HEC export, safe structured logging with no payloads, air-gap bundles with all artifacts and provenance, Prometheus/Grafana dashboards that are curated and work out-of-box, and alerting aligned to SLO burn rates with low noise and embedded runbooks.

[35:16] Priya Desai: Yes.

[35:18] Kevin Lau: Yes.

[35:19] Monica Reyes: Yep.

[35:21] Markus Klein: Next steps from Redwood: we’ll send the dashboard catalog and alert set, the export integration matrix, and draft air-gap artifact list. Colin will follow with Splunk HEC config example. Fatima will outline offline packaging and verification assumptions. Logan will confirm what sinks are supported in the initial minimum pack.

[35:45] Markus Klein: Next steps from Harrington: Monica, confirm SIEM ingest preferences and required fields. Kevin, share SLO/paging policy and what’s page-worthy. Priya, confirm whether we can run a small in-cluster Prometheus in on-prem or if it must integrate with your central tools.

[36:08] Priya Desai: Yep, we can do that.

[36:10] Kevin Lau: Sure.

[36:11] Monica Reyes: Yep.

[36:13] Markus Klein: Great. We’ll schedule a follow-up, probably 45 minutes, with your SecOps and Platform folks to review redaction and retention. Sound good?

[36:22] Priya Desai: Works.

[36:24] Markus Klein: Cool. Thanks everyone.

[36:27] (meeting ends in recording shortly after; minor tail)

Questions captured (auto-generated)
- Do operational logs include prompts/completions by default? (No)
- What log sinks are supported in v1 minimum pack (Splunk HEC prioritized; Elastic/Loki likely; OTLP TBD)
- How are air-gapped artifacts packaged, verified, and upgraded/rolled back?
- How are alert severities mapped to paging vs ticketing, and are runbook links embedded?

title: Go/No-Go: Dedicated staged rollouts (beta) + incident review + launch checklist
preview: Internal go/no-go for enabling Dedicated staged rollouts for a limited beta (1–2 design partners). Reviewed early beta failures (429 spike due to canary partition starving effective capacity, gate evidence missing due to telemetry tag mismatch, and metrics cardinality regression). Aligning on ship criteria: feature flag default OFF, validated rollback/abort semantics, stable gate evaluation under telemetry lag, and audit export readiness. Decision: GO for limited beta with guardrails and explicit hold/force-promote controls restricted to admin roles; no broad enablement until SIEM export and small-pool safeguards are verified.

They are seeing expected cost reductions in variant B but are confused by several Console dashboard behaviors (time window alignment, baseline comparison, and what triggers a guardrail badge). Redwood clarified current semantics (soft-stop defaults, guardrail evaluation windows, known propagation lag and attribution drop issues) and agreed on changes: clearer UI copy/tooltips for deltas, making the evaluation window explicit, and tightening guidance around stickiness keys and streaming retry attribution. title: POC check-in - stability + rollout plan (Acme AI)
preview:
Fireflies.ai
Meeting Transcript

Title: POC check-in - stability + rollout plan (Acme AI)
Date: 2025-02-25
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 43 min

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Rachel Kim, Liam O'Rourke, Noah Patel, Rafael Mendes
Acme AI: Priya Shah, Daniel Cho, Maya Chen, Alex Nguyen

Summary (auto-generated)
- Reviewed results from last week’s mitigations (concurrency cap + batch sizing guardrails) and early canary signals for TP stability fixes.
- Discussed runtime 1.19 rollout sequencing: Dedicated canary → expanded canary → production.
- Aligned on success criteria for Acme: reduced request timeouts, no “stuck stream” behavior, and stable p99 latency under mixed prefill/decode.
- Identified remaining risk areas: client-cancel path during streaming, NCCL stall signatures, and false positive watchdog aborts.

Topics (auto-generated)
1) Current state: timeout rate and p99 since mitigations
2) Canary readout: what improved / what still looks weird
3) Rollout plan and feature flags (watchdog, scheduler guardrail)
4) Success criteria + what data Redwood needs from Acme
5) Next steps / go-no go

Questions (auto-generated)
- Will the watchdog abort cause customer-visible errors? Is there retry behavior?
- What is the rollback trigger if p99 regresses but timeouts go down?
- Can Acme run with extra NCCL debug logging safely?

Transcript

[00:00] Rachel Kim: Cool, I think we’re recording. Um thanks everyone for jumping on. This is our follow-up from last week’s deep dive on the TP hangs. Goal today is basically: (1) are the mitigations helping, (2) what did we see in the canary, and (3) agree on rollout timeline and success criteria.

[00:18] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:20] Liam O'Rourke: Hey all.

[00:22] Noah Patel: Hi.

[00:23] Rafael Mendes: Hey, Rafael from SRE.

[00:26] Daniel Cho: Daniel here.

[00:27] Alex Nguyen: Alex, ML.

[00:29] Maya Chen: Maya.

[00:31] Rachel Kim: Great. Priya, do you want to start with like, what you’ve seen since you applied the concurrency cap and the smaller batch? Like qualitatively and numbers if you have them.

[00:41] Priya Shah: Yeah. So we turned on the caps Friday night, basically we put the per-replica concurrency down… I think from 24 to 14? Daniel can correct me. And we also set the max prefill tokens per batch, and that reduced the like, “everything is stuck” events.

[00:59] Daniel Cho: Yeah, it was 24 to 12 actually on the busiest deployment. We did 14 on the less busy one. And the timeouts, I pulled the graph this morning. The request timeout rate dropped from around 0.8% at peak to like 0.15-ish. It’s not zero, but it’s like 5x better.

[01:17] Rachel Kim: That’s good.

[01:18] Daniel Cho: But p99 got a little worse, which makes sense because we’re throttling. Like p99 went from ~3.8 seconds to ~4.6 seconds on the chat endpoint.

[01:28] Maya Chen: Which is okay-ish for us for now, but not like… we can’t keep it there forever.

[01:34] Rachel Kim: Yeah totally. The point of the mitigation is “stop the bleeding” not the steady state.

[01:39] Noah Patel: And the pattern you’re describing matches what we’ve seen. The hang signature is triggered under a certain mix, so reducing concurrency reduces probability, but it also increases queuing.

[01:50] Priya Shah: Right.

[01:51] Rachel Kim: So that’s the baseline. Now, on our side, we shipped a canary build to a small set of Dedicated clusters last week with the scheduling guardrail and additional NCCL stream sync. We haven’t rolled it broad on Hosted yet. For Acme, we can do a canary on one replica group, TP=4, A100 80 gig, same as production.

[02:12] Alex Nguyen: That’s the same hardware we have, yeah.

[02:15] Rachel Kim: Noah, do you want to summarize what changed in the canary, in plain English.

[02:20] Noah Patel: Sure. So, three buckets. One: scheduler changes so collectives can’t be starved by prefill-heavy batches. Before, under certain interleavings, rank 0 could get ahead on compute and then wait on a collective that other ranks never reach because they’re stuck doing other work, and it looks like a “silent stall.” Two: stricter stream/event ordering around NCCL calls; some paths relied on implicit sync, which is not always safe especially with multi-stream decode. Three: instrumentation—heartbeat per rank, in-flight collective counters, and stall reason tags. And then the optional thing, which we haven’t defaulted on, is the watchdog timeout that aborts the step and drains the replica instead of waiting forever.

[03:04] Daniel Cho: When you say drains the replica, does that mean it kills the pod?

[03:08] Rafael Mendes: Not necessarily kill, but it removes it from service and we replace it. Practically, yes, you’ll see a drain event and then a restart depending on the environment policy.

[03:17] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[03:18] Maya Chen: And does that mean end-user sees an error?

[03:21] Rachel Kim: Potentially, yes, but a fast error rather than a 60-second hang then timeout. And we can route or retry at your client layer depending how you’re set up.

[03:30] Noah Patel: The idea is “fail closed” with a structured error code. It should be actionable: “tp_stall_watchdog_abort” basically, rather than generic 504.

[03:40] Priya Shah: That’s better than the long wait. We can handle retries if it’s not too frequent.

[03:45] Rachel Kim: Exactly. So, canary results so far. Rafael, do you want to share what SRE saw on the Dedicated canary clusters?

[03:53] Rafael Mendes: Yep. On the two clusters we canaried—different customers, similar workloads—we saw timeouts drop materially. On one, request timeout rate during peak went from ~0.6% to ~0.05%. The big thing is the heartbeat gaps basically disappeared. We still saw occasional NCCL errors, but those are now surfaced and correlate with a drain event. So instead of the cluster getting into a wedged state for like 20 minutes, it recovers in, say, under 2 minutes.

[04:23] Daniel Cho: That’s good.

[04:24] Rafael Mendes: Perf impact: there was a small regression, like 1 to 2% throughput down, because of extra sync. But it’s within our error bars and, honestly, worth it for stability.

[04:36] Alex Nguyen: And did you run long context? We have like 16k prompts sometimes.

[04:41] Noah Patel: We did. The canary included long-context stress scenario. The hang signature was more likely with mixed long prefill and then streaming decode on other requests. With the scheduler guardrail, we didn’t reproduce the “decode never returns” issue.

[04:56] Priya Shah: So you’re saying it’s fixed.

[04:58] Noah Patel: I’m saying the top two signatures we’re targeting appear mitigated. I won’t say “all hangs are fixed” because TP is… you know. But the known failure modes are improved and we have detection now.

[05:10] Rachel Kim: Priya, Maya, what you care about is: will your customer-facing timeouts stop. Our confidence is higher now.

[05:18] Maya Chen: Ok. What’s the rollout timeline if we agree to canary.

[05:23] Rachel Kim: Proposal: we enable canary for you tomorrow on one replica group—like 10% of traffic or one deployment slice—run it for 24 hours including a peak. If results look good Thursday, we expand to 50% Friday, then full production early next week. That’s assuming no freeze window on your side.

[05:44] Priya Shah: We do have a release freeze starting next Thursday for our own product. So ideally we’d like it in before that or we wait.

[05:52] Rachel Kim: Got it. So we aim to be in prod by Monday/Tuesday.

[05:56] Daniel Cho: How risky is this? Like what’s the rollback plan.

[06:00] Rafael Mendes: Rollback is straightforward: we can revert the runtime version for that deployment slice and/or disable the feature flags. The biggest question is if the watchdog causes false positives and drains too aggressively.

[06:12] Daniel Cho: Right, that’s what I’m worried about. If it drains constantly, we’ll get flapping.

[06:17] Noah Patel: That’s why we don’t want it default-on at first. We can start with instrumentation + scheduler + sync, and leave watchdog off. Then if you still have a small residual hang rate, we can enable watchdog with conservative thresholds.

[06:32] Maya Chen: So without watchdog, you think timeouts go away?

[06:35] Noah Patel: For the hang signatures we saw, yes. Watchdog is more like a safety net for unknowns.

[06:41] Priya Shah: Ok. That seems reasonable.

[06:44] Rachel Kim: Daniel, when you say timeouts still exist at 0.15%… do you know if they are all the same pattern? Like do you see GPU stall, or could be client cancellations.

[06:55] Daniel Cho: That’s actually unclear. Some are client disconnect, because streaming. But some are server side 504s. We didn’t have enough instrumentation to tell if it’s NCCL or scheduler.

[07:06] Noah Patel: With the new metrics, you’ll be able to tell. Like you’ll see “stall_reason=comms” vs “stall_reason=scheduler_starvation” vs “compute_long” etc.

[07:15] Alex Nguyen: Are those in the console or do we have to scrape Prometheus.

[07:19] Rachel Kim: For Dedicated, you’ll see them in the Redwood Console dashboards we share with you, and we can also export. Rafael can confirm the exact panels.

[07:27] Rafael Mendes: Yeah, we can add you to the dashboard view. There’s a panel for “TP heartbeat gap seconds” and another for “in-flight collectives” which is basically a counter per rank. If one rank’s counter stops moving, that’s the smoking gun.

[07:42] Daniel Cho: Perfect.

[07:43] Maya Chen: Can we talk success criteria. Like what do we agree means “go.”

[07:48] Rachel Kim: Yes. I drafted something but let’s align. My suggestion: during peak hour window, request timeout rate below 0.05% on the canary slice, and no sustained heartbeat gaps over, say, 30 seconds. Also p99 not worse than today’s mitigated baseline, ideally improves when we relax concurrency caps.

[08:08] Priya Shah: That’s fine. We also care about “stuck streams,” like the client waiting and then nothing.

[08:14] Noah Patel: Those should show up as heartbeat gaps or as “decode step exceeded” if watchdog is on.

[08:20] Daniel Cho: For p99, I’d like to get back under 4 seconds. The cap pushes it above.

[08:25] Rachel Kim: Yep. And part of the plan is: after stability is improved, we can increase concurrency gradually. We don’t need to go back to 24 immediately.

[08:36] Alex Nguyen: Also we need to confirm no quality changes. This is runtime not model, but we had one case with another vendor where tokens got weird.

[08:44] Noah Patel: This shouldn’t change model outputs; it’s scheduling and synchronization. But if you see anything odd, let us know.

[08:53] Maya Chen: Ok.

[08:55] Rachel Kim: Quick question: are you still doing TP=4 for all models? Or do you have a TP=2 slice.

[09:01] Priya Shah: For production it’s TP=4, single replica group per region. We tested TP=2 but throughput wasn’t enough.

[09:10] Rachel Kim: Ok.

[09:11] Daniel Cho: Another question: you mentioned extra NCCL logging. Do we need that for the canary.

[09:18] Rafael Mendes: Not unless we see something new. We can enable it for a short window if there’s an incident, but it’s noisy and has some overhead. We’ve been trying to keep it minimal.

[09:30] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[09:31] Rachel Kim: Let’s talk about the canary setup. Liam, do you want to cover how we route traffic.

[09:36] Liam O'Rourke: Sure. We can do it a couple ways. Easiest is: new runtime version on one deployment slice, then route a fixed percentage of requests by a hash. We can also do “shadow” but that’s more complicated. For what we’re doing—stability—you want real traffic.

[09:54] Priya Shah: Real traffic, yes.

[09:56] Liam O'Rourke: So we’ll do 10% by hash to canary, and you’ll be able to compare metrics side-by-side.

[10:04] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[10:06] Rachel Kim: If you’re good with it, we can start tomorrow morning your time.

[10:10] Priya Shah: Works.

[10:11] Maya Chen: Can you also share in writing what exactly changed. Like release notes.

[10:16] Noah Patel: Yeah. It’s basically: scheduler guardrail to prevent collective starvation; NCCL stream/event sync hardening; new metrics; and improved error surfacing. Watchdog is behind a flag.

[10:30] Rachel Kim: I’ll send a recap email with those bullets and the success criteria.

[10:35] Priya Shah: Great.

[10:36] Daniel Cho: I want to ask about the client cancel thing. We had some customers cancel requests in the UI. Could that trigger the hang.

[10:45] Noah Patel: Yes, we found a cancellation path bug in TP where one rank could exit early and another rank still expects a collective. That’s one of the fixes in this line of work. I believe it’s in the 1.19 bundle.

[10:59] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[11:00] Alex Nguyen: So do we need to change our client behavior.

[11:03] Noah Patel: Ideally no. Cancellation should be safe. But if you’re aggressively cancelling every token, that’s… unusual. How often do you see cancels.

[11:12] Daniel Cho: It’s like user navigates away, or they hit stop generation.

[11:16] Noah Patel: That should be fine.

[11:19] Rachel Kim: On the timeouts: you mentioned 0.15% after caps. If we canary tomorrow and see it drop further without raising p99, that’s a win.

[11:31] Maya Chen: Do we plan to remove the caps immediately.

[11:34] Rachel Kim: Not immediately. We’ll keep them during canary for apples-to-apples. Then once canary is stable, we raise concurrency in steps. Like 12 → 14 → 16, watching the stall metrics.

[11:47] Priya Shah: That’s sensible.

[11:50] Rafael Mendes: And from on-call perspective, we’ll also have new alerts. So if there is a stall signal, we drain the replica proactively instead of waiting for customer timeouts.

[12:01] Daniel Cho: That’s the piece we wanted.

[12:04] Rachel Kim: Any concerns from your side about doing the canary tomorrow.

[12:09] Priya Shah: Only the freeze timeline. But if we’re in prod by Monday, that’s fine.

[12:13] Maya Chen: Yeah.

[12:15] Alex Nguyen: We’ll need access to the dashboard.

[12:17] Rafael Mendes: I’ll coordinate that with you after this call.

[12:20] Rachel Kim: Ok. I also want to set expectations: even with the fixes, you may see an occasional drain event. But it should be rare and not cascade.

[12:30] Daniel Cho: Define rare.

[12:32] Noah Patel: In the canary we saw like, single digits per day at worst, and those were mostly correlated with real NCCL errors rather than stalls. The stalls went down drastically.

[12:44] Priya Shah: Ok.

[12:45] Maya Chen: And if we see drain events, does that show as a brief blip.

[12:49] Liam O'Rourke: If you have enough replicas, it’s masked. If you have one replica group with limited redundancy, you might see a small latency spike. But better than a long wedge.

[13:02] Priya Shah: Understood.

[13:04] Rachel Kim: Let’s do a quick recap of what we need from Acme during the canary. Daniel, we’ll ask for: request timeout rate, p95/p99, and if possible count of client cancels.

[13:16] Daniel Cho: Yes.

[13:17] Rachel Kim: And then if any errors happen, please paste the structured error code and request id so we can correlate.

[13:24] Daniel Cho: Ok.

[13:26] Rafael Mendes: Also if you can note when your peak traffic is—like hour window—so we can eyeball the graphs at the same time.

[13:34] Daniel Cho: Peak is 9am to 11am Pacific.

[13:38] Rafael Mendes: Great.

[13:39] Rachel Kim: Cool.

[13:41] Maya Chen: One more question: do you think this solves the enterprise customer impact we had last week. Like are we still at risk of a sev1.

[13:49] Noah Patel: Risk is reduced significantly. The main issue was indefinite stalls in TP that led to timeouts and autoscaling thrash. With ordering fixes and instrumentation, we can prevent and detect it. Watchdog is the backstop.

[14:05] Priya Shah: Ok.

[14:06] Rachel Kim: There’s also the question of rolling this to Hosted eventually, but for you this is Dedicated. We’ll do Dedicated-first.

[14:13] Maya Chen: Good.

[14:15] Rachel Kim: Alright, I’m going to skip ahead a bit—Noah, anything else you want them to know about the canary behavior.

[14:21] Noah Patel: Only that if you see a small throughput regression, like 1-2%, it’s expected due to stream sync. We can try to conditionalize later, but for now stability > micro perf.

[14:33] Alex Nguyen: Ok.

[14:34] Priya Shah: That’s fine.

[14:36] Rachel Kim: Great. Let’s do next steps and then we can give you time back.

[14:41] Rachel Kim: Next steps: I send recap today with success criteria and schedule. Tomorrow: canary enabled 10% with the 1.19 build, watchdog off by default. Thursday: review results. Friday: expand if good. Monday: full rollout pending go/no-go.

[14:59] Priya Shah: Sounds right.

[15:01] Daniel Cho: Works.

[15:03] Rafael Mendes: I’ll make sure you have dashboard access and I’ll be on point during your peak window tomorrow.

[15:08] Maya Chen: Thank you.

[15:10] Liam O'Rourke: I’ll coordinate the actual traffic split change with our release folks.

[15:14] Rachel Kim: Awesome. Any last questions.

[15:17] Daniel Cho: Not from me.

[15:18] Priya Shah: All good.

[15:20] Maya Chen: Thanks.

[15:21] Rachel Kim: Great, thanks everyone.

[15:24] (Call continues with a few minutes of scheduling and side chatter; transcript ends.)

Notes
- Acme confirmed current mitigations (lower concurrency + batch guardrails) reduced timeouts ~5x but increased p99 latency.
- Redwood proposed canary tomorrow; focus on stability fixes without enabling watchdog initially.
- Agreed success criteria: timeout rate <0.05% at peak on canary slice, no sustained TP heartbeat gaps, and path to bring p99 back <4s by raising concurrency after stability is proven.

[11:16] Liam O'Rourke: We’ll send this as “temporary config” and you can approve for your Dedicated deployment.

[11:22] Priya Nair: Okay.

[11:24] Rafael Mendes: For the incident windows: besides Feb 17, do you have another?

[11:29] Priya Nair: Feb 18 around 01:45 UTC. Similar pattern.

[11:34] Rafael Mendes: Great. Same ask: logs + IDs.

[11:43] Priya Nair: Yes. We had a deploy and some clients were reconnecting. So yeah, likely.

- Follow-up meeting after artifact review

[01:01] Hiro Tanaka: Got it. Is GPU utilization flat during the hang, or pegged?

[01:05] Ethan Brooks: Flat. We see a drop. It’s not like it’s doing compute slowly. It’s like stalled.

[01:36] Rafael Mendes: And you’re seeing it under sustained load, or only during spikes?

[01:41] Priya Nair: Mostly spikes. Like when we get bursty traffic, it starts. But we also saw it last night under steady load with a long-context job.

[01:52] Rachel Kim: Can you share a rough frequency? Like once a day, once a week?

[01:57] Priya Nair: Last week it was twice. Since Sunday, it’s been like… daily. We had a bigger launch.
